accusative-case.title: Unit 5: The Accusative Case & Basic Prepositions
accusative-case.content: Unit 5: The Accusative Case & Basic Prepositions In Unit 2, you learned that Greek nouns change their shape depending on their role in the sentence. When a noun is the target of an action, it enters the Accusative Case . This case is the workhorse of conversational Greek. Whenever you see, have, want, or go to something, you will use the Accusative. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the "Object Case," the masculine "N" rule, and the three fundamental prepositions: Σε, Από, and Με. 1. The Golden Rule of the Object If you want to say "I see George" (Βλέπω τον Γιώργο) or "I have the book" (Έχω το βιβλίο), the object receiving the action must transform: Masculine Nouns: The article ο becomes τον . The noun drops its final -ς . (Ο φίλος -> τον φίλο) Feminine Nouns: The article η becomes τη(ν) . The noun itself does not change . (Η φίλη -> τη(ν) φίλη) Neuter Nouns: Total freebie! Both the article το and the noun remain exactly the same . (Το βιβλίο -> το βιβλίο) IMPORTANT The Masculine "N" Rule In writing, we always keep the final -ν in the masculine article ( τον, έναν ). This prevents it from being confused with the neuter article ( το, ένα ). CAUTION The "Mirror" Exception Most verbs force the next noun into the Accusative case. But the verb Είμαι (To Be) acts like a mirror—it reflects the subject. Therefore, whatever comes after Είμαι stays in the Subject (Nominative) case! Correct: Είμαι ο Γιώργος. (I am George). Wrong: Είμαι τον Γιώργο. 2. The "Big Three" Prepositions Prepositions are tiny directional words like "to," "from," and "with." The three most important prepositions in Greek force the noun that follows them into the Accusative case . A. Σε (To / At / In / On) This power-word covers almost all locations and destinations. In Greek, we almost never say "σε" and "the" separately. They merge into a single super-word: σε + τον = στον (to the / at the - masculine) σε + την = στη (ν) (to the / at the - feminine) σε + το = στο (to the / at the - neuter) Examples: Πάω στο σινεμά. (I am going to the cinema / I go to the cinema). Το κινητό είναι στο τραπέζι. (The phone is on the table). B. Από (From / By) Used for origins or the starting point of time and space. Unlike σε , it does not physically merge with the article in writing, but it still forces the Accusative case. Είμαι από την Ελλάδα. (I am from Greece). TIP Blend it! Even though από is written separately, Greeks speak fast. The final 'ο' of από is often swallowed before a vowel. " Από την Αθήνα " sounds like " Ap-tin Athína ". C. Με (With) Used for company, instruments, or modes of transport. Θέλω καφέ με ζάχαρη. (I want coffee with sugar). Πάμε με το λεωφορείο; (Are we going by bus?) 3. Pronunciation Masterclass: The Disappearing 'N' While the masculine article ( τον, στον ) always keeps its -ν in writing, the feminine article ( την, στην ) is picky. When to KEEP the 'N': You only keep the liquid 'ν' if the next word starts with a strong or open sound: 1. Any Vowel (α, ε, η, ι, ο, υ, ω) 2. Hard Consonants (κ, π, τ, ξ, ψ) 3. Double Consonants (μπ, ντ, γκ, τσ, τζ) Στην πόρτα (At the door - Kept because 'π' is hard). When to DROP the 'N': If the next word starts with a soft, continuous sound (like β, δ, γ, φ, θ, χ, μ, ν, λ, ρ, σ, ζ), drop the 'ν' in both speech and writing! Στη θάλασσα (At the sea). 4. The Blending Consonants (Speaking like a Native) When the final 'ν' (from τον, την, δεν ) crashes into a hard consonant ( κ, π, τ ), a phonetic magic trick happens! ν + π = mb: τον πατέρα sounds like to(m)-batéra . ν + τ = nd: στον τρελό sounds like sto(n)-dreló . ν + κ = ng: την Κυριακή sounds like ti(ng)-giriakí . (Don't stress over this immediately. It happens naturally as you speed up your speech!) 🎯 Unit 5 Mastery Checklist I can drop the final -ς from masculine nouns when they are the object. I always keep the -ν in τον so I don't confuse masculine and neuter. I know how to combine σε with articles to make στον, στην, στο . I know to drop the feminine -ν before soft letters like θ, φ, or μ. I can pronounce the mb, nd, ng blends when ν meets π, τ, κ . Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Essential Action Verbs Next Lesson: Unit 6: Adjectives, Colors & Numbers

adjective-comparison.title: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison (Better, Faster)
adjective-comparison.content: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison (Better, Faster) In Greek, just like in English, there are two ways to compare things (e.g., "tall" ➔ "taller"). You can use the easy "more" method, or you can use the specialized one-word "suffix" method. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the "Πιο" method vs. the "-τερος" suffix, the stress rule for comparison, and the "Absolute Superlative" (-τατος). 1. The Easy Way: The "Πιο" Method This is the most common way to speak in modern everyday Greek. You simply put the word πιο (more) right in front of the normal adjective. Μεγάλος (Big) ➔ Πιο μεγάλος (Bigger). Ωραία (Beautiful) ➔ Πιο ωραία (More beautiful). Ακριβό (Expensive) ➔ Πιο ακριβό (More expensive). How to say "than" To complete the comparison (e.g., "taller than ..."), Greek uses the preposition από followed by the Accusative case. Ο Γιώργος είναι πιο ψηλός από τον Νίκο . (George is taller than Nikos ). Το κρασί είναι πιο ακριβό από το νερό . (Wine is more expensive than water ). TIP Native Secret: The Genitive Shortcut If you want to say "than me" or "than you," you can use από μένα . But native speakers often completely drop the "από" and just attach a weak possessive pronoun (Genitive) directly to the end of the adjective! Η Άννα είναι μικρότερή μου . (Anna is younger than me / my junior). Είναι ψηλότερός σου . (He is taller than you ). 2. The Native Way: The "-τερος" Suffix If you want to sound more elegant, faster, or more formal, you can form a one-word comparative by replacing the adjective's normal ending with the suffix -τερος, -τερη, -τερο . The Stress Rule: When you use this suffix, the stress mark is heavily anchored: it must fall on the third syllable from the end! Ψηλός (Tall) ➔ Ψηλότερος (Taller). Νέος (Young) ➔ Νεότερος (Younger). Σπουδαίος (Important) ➔ Σπουδαιότερος (More important). WARNING The "Πιο" Only Rule You cannot put the -τερος suffix on everything! You absolutely MUST use the easy πιο method for: 1. Passive Participles: Adjectives ending in -μένος ( πιο κουρασμένος - more tired). 2. Strange Vowels: Adjectives ending in -ης ( πιο συνεπής - more consistent). 3. Foreign Colors: Indeclinable words like ροζ or μπλε ( πιο ροζ - more pink). 3. The Superlative: "The Best" To say "the most" or "the best," you don't need a new word. You simply add the Definite Article ( ο, η, το ) to the very front of the comparative phrase! Ο πιο καλός / Ο καλύτερος (The best). Η πιο ωραία / Η ωραιότερη (The most beautiful). Το ψηλότερο κτήριο. (The tallest building). 4. The Absolute Superlative: "Extremely" If you don't want to compare something to anything else, but just want to emphasize that it is "extremely" or "unbelievably" X, you use the absolute superlative suffix: -τατος, -τατη, -τατο . (The stress is also on the third syllable from the end!) Ωραιότατος (Extremely beautiful / Gorgeous). Ακριβότατος (Unbelievably expensive). Φθηνότατος (Super cheap). 5. The Top Irregulars Just like English ("good" ➔ "better", not "gooder"), the most common adjectives in Greek refuse to follow the rules. You must memorize these changelings: The Adjective Comparative (Better/More) Absolute (Extremely/Optimal) Καλός (Good) καλύτερος άριστος (Excellent) Κακός (Bad) χειρότερος χείριστος / κάκιστος (The worst possible) Πολύς (Much/Many) περισσότερος πλείστος (Vast majority) Μεγάλος (Big) μεγαλύτερος μέγιστος (Maximum) Μικρός (Small) μικρότερος ελάχιστος (Minimum / Tiny) Λίγος (Little) λιγότερος (Less) ελάχιστος (Hardly any) 🎯 Unit 25 Mastery Checklist I can use πιο + adjective to compare things easily. I can build the one-word comparative using the -τερος suffix, remembering the stress rule. I know how to use από + Accusative, or the Native Shortcut (Genitive pronoun) for "than". I know that adding the definite article ( ο, η, το ) turns a comparative into a superlative ("the most"). I can use the -τατος suffix to say something is "extremely" X. I have memorized the essential irregulars ( καλύτερος, χειρότερος, μεγαλύτερος ). Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Adjectives & Comparisons Next Lesson: Unit 26: Building Participles (-οντας, -μένος)

adjectives.title: Unit 6: Adjectives, Colors & Numbers
adjectives.content: Unit 6: Adjectives, Colors & Numbers In English, adjectives (words that describe things) are completely static. A "good" man, a "good" woman, a "good" idea. The word never changes. In Greek, adjectives are chameleons . They must perfectly match the noun they are describing in Gender , Number , and Case . This powerful concept is called Agreement . TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the "Chameleon Rule," agreement, and the invariant colors and numbers 1-10. 1. The Chameleon Rule Every Greek adjective in the dictionary has three versions (Masculine, Feminine, and Neuter). It acts exactly like a noun! You simply pick the version that matches the noun you want to describe. Notice how the endings of the adjective often perfectly "rhyme" with the article and the noun: Gender Subject (Nominative) Object (Accusative) Translation Masculine Ο καλ ός φίλ ος Τον καλ ό φίλ ο The good friend (m) Feminine Η καλ ή φίλ η Τη(ν) καλ ή φίλ η The good friend (f) Neuter Το καλ ό παιδ ί Το καλ ό παιδ ί The good child TIP Adjectives Drop the 'ς' Too! Did you notice the Accusative masculine form? Just like masculine nouns drop their final -ς when they become the object (ο φίλος -> τον φίλο), the adjective must do exactly the same (ο καλός -> τον καλό). They hunt in packs! 2. Painting the World: Colors (Τα Χρώματα) Colors are just adjectives. They are the perfect way to practice Agreement because you use them every day. Κόκκινος, κόκκινη, κόκκινο (Red) -> Το κόκκινο μήλο (The red apple) Μαύρος, μαύρη, μαύρο (Black) -> Ο μαύρος σκύλος (The black dog) Πράσινος, πράσινη, πράσινο (Green) -> Η πράσινη πόρτα (The green door) CAUTION The Rebel Colors A few colors borrowed from other languages refuse to change their shape! Μπλε (Blue), Ροζ (Pink), and Καφέ (Brown) stay exactly the same regardless of what they describe! Ο μπλε ουρανός, η μπλε θάλασσα, το μπλε αυτοκίνητο. (All correct!) 3. Counting 1 to 10 (Αριθμοί 1-10) Learning to count is the key to daily survival, shopping, and telling time. Ένας / Μία / Ένα (One) Δύο (Two) Τρία (Three) Τέσσερα (Four) Πέντε (Five) Έξι (Six) Εφτά / Επτά (Seven) Οχτώ / Οκτώ (Eight) Εννέα / Εννιά (Nine) Δέκα (Ten) IMPORTANT Number One is a Chameleon! The number "One" is the exact same word as the indefinite articles (a/an) you learned in Unit 2! It must match the gender of what you are counting: Ένας άντρας (One man) Μία γυναίκα (One woman) Ένα παιδί (One child) (Fun Fact: The numbers 3 and 4 are also mini-chameleons! You will learn their masculine/feminine forms later, but for now, use Τρία and Τέσσερα when counting out loud.) 🎯 Unit 6 Mastery Checklist I understand the concept of "Agreement" between an article, adjective, and noun. I can drop the final -ς from both the masculine adjective and noun when they are the target of the action. I have memorized the numbers 1 to 10. I know that μπλε , ροζ , and καφέ never change their endings. Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Colors & Numbers Next Lesson: Unit 7: Asking Questions

adverbs.title: Unit 17: Adverbs & Expressions of Manner
adverbs.content: Unit 17: Adverbs & Expressions of Manner In Unit 6, you learned that Adjectives are "chameleons"—they constantly change their shape to match the noun they describe. Adverbs , on the other hand, are the easiest words in the Greek language. They are completely "frozen." They exist to describe how, where, when, or how much an action happens, and they never change their shape . TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the "-α" trick, the difference between "Πότε" and "Ποτέ", and the beauty of double negatives. 1. Adverbs of Manner: How? (Πώς;) These adverbs describe the "vibe" or the method of an action. TIP The "-α" Trick You can turn almost any regular adjective into an adverb simply by changing its ending to -α (which looks exactly like the neuter plural form!). Καλός (Good) ➔ Καλά (Well). e.g., Διαβάζει καλά. (He reads well). Γρήγορος (Fast) ➔ Γρήγορα (Quickly). e.g., Τρέχει γρήγορα. (She runs fast). Ωραίος (Nice/Beautiful) ➔ Ωραία (Nicely/Beautifully). Here are some other highly common adverbs of manner: Σιγά (Slowly / Quietly) Μαζί (Together) Έτσι (Like this / So / Thus) 2. Adverbs of Place: Where? (Πού;) Use these to describe the physical location or direction of an action. Εδώ (Here) Εκεί (There) Πάνω (Up / On top) Κάτω (Down / Below) Μέσα (Inside) Έξω (Outside) (Native speakers constantly pair these with the preposition από : e.g., Έξω από το σπίτι - Outside of the house). 3. Adverbs of Time: When? (Πότε;) These are your anchors for deciding which tense to use! Τώρα (Now) Σήμερα (Today) Αύριο (Tomorrow) Χθες / Χτες (Yesterday) Πάντα (Always) Ποτέ (Never) CAUTION Spelling Alert: Πότε vs. Ποτέ The meaning of the word completely changes depending on where you put the stress! Πότε; (Accent on the first syllable) = When? (e.g., Πότε φεύγεις;) Ποτέ (Accent on the last syllable) = Never. (e.g., Δεν πίνω ποτέ. - Notice that Greek uses a double negative: "I don't drink never"). 4. Adverbs of Quantity: How much? (Πόσο;) These adverbs tell you the degree or intensity of an action. Πολύ (Very / A lot / Much) Λίγο (A little) Αρκετά (Enough / Quite) Καθόλου (Not at all / At all) NOTE The Double Negative Combo Just like ποτέ , when you use the word καθόλου as a negative statement, you usually pair it with δεν ! Δεν μου αρέσει καθόλου. (I don't like it at all). Δεν ξέρω καθόλου. (I don't know at all). 🎯 Unit 17 Mastery Checklist I understand that adverbs are "frozen" and never change their endings to match nouns. I know the "-α" trick to turn adjectives into adverbs ( Καλός ➔ Καλά ). I can pronounce the difference between Πότε (When?) and Ποτέ (Never). I remember that Greek loves double negatives ( Δεν θέλω ποτέ , Δεν τρώω καθόλου ). Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Directions & Commands Next Lesson: Unit 18: Deponent Verbs (Passive Intro)

ancient-remnants.title: Unit 31: Ancient Remnants & Formal Registers
ancient-remnants.content: Ancient Remnants in Modern Greek Welcome to Level C2: Mastery. Modern Greek is a living, breathing language, but it carries the ghosts of its 3,000-year history everywhere it goes. In the 19th and 20th centuries, Greece had two official languages: 1. Demotic (Δημοτική): The natural spoken language of the people. 2. Katharevousa (Καθαρεύουσα): An artificial, highly purified, "ancient-sounding" language used by the government, schools, and news anchors. In 1976, Demotic officially won and became the sole language of Greece. However, the 150 years of Katharevousa left deep scars on the modern language. To achieve true C2 fluency, you must understand the "Ancient Remnants" that are still used in the news, literature, and formal speech today. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on frozen Dative phrases like "Εν τω μεταξύ", formal prepositions (Genitive), and the 3rd declension feminine nouns. 1. Dative Expressions (The Lost Case) Ancient Greek had a 5th case: The Dative Case (used to show "to" or "for" someone). Modern Greek replaced this entirely by using "σε + Accusative". However, dozens of Dative phrases survived as frozen, highly formal expressions. You must memorize them as whole blocks: Εν τω μεταξύ (In the meantime) Δόξα τω Θεώ (Glory to God / Thank God) Εν πάση περιπτώσει (In any case / Anyway) Εν τέλει (In the end / Finally) Τοις μετρητοίς (In cash) NOTE The tiny word εν (in) is an ancient preposition that ALWAYS triggers the Dative case. You will see it constantly in C2 vocabulary! 2. Ancient Prepositions C2 Greek resurrects several ancient prepositions that demand the Genitive case . They are used in formal contexts, journalism, and academic writing. Ancient Preposition Meaning Modern Equivalent Example Ενώπιον (+ Gen) Before / In the presence of Μπροστά σε Ορκίστηκε ενώπιον του δικαστηρίου. Λόγω (+ Gen) Due to / Because of Επειδή / Εξαιτίας Κλειστό λόγω βροχής. Μέσω (+ Gen) Via / Through Από Έφτασα μέσω Αθηνών. Άνευ (+ Gen) Without Χωρίς Διασταύρωση άνευ σηματοδότη. Κατά (+ Gen) Against Εναντίον Διαδήλωση κατά της κυβέρνησης. 3. The 3rd Declension Nouns (Singular -εως / Plural -εις) Katharevousa dragged a group of ancient "3rd Declension" nouns into modern Greek. These are almost always abstract feminine nouns ending in -η (ancient -ις ). You will recognize them because they almost all end in -ση, -ξη, or -ψη ! Their defining feature is their formal Genitive singular ending: -εως . Η πόλη (The city) ➔ Το κέντρο της πόλεως (The center of the city). Η απόφαση (The decision) ➔ Η λήψη της αποφάσεως (The taking of the decision). IMPORTANT The Accent Shift Rule: If the word has more than two syllables (like κυβέρνηση ), the stress must move one syllable forward in the formal -εως genitive (➔ κυβερνήσεως ). If it has only two syllables (like πόλη ), the stress stays in the same place ( πόλεως ). The Plural (-εις): In the plural , these nouns ALWAYS use the ancient endings: Nominative/Accusative -εις and Genitive -εων . Οι αποφάσεις (The decisions) ➔ Των αποφάσεων . Οι πόλεις (The cities) ➔ Των πόλεων . 4. Bonus: C2 Adjectives & Participles To truly master formal Greek, you must recognize two ancient adjective classes that survive perfectly intact: The -ης / -ες Adjectives: These do not have separate forms for masculine and feminine. In the Genitive singular (for all genders), they take the ancient ending -ους . ακριβής (exact/precise), διεθνής (international), συνεχής (continuous). Genitive: Το αεροδρόμιο του διεθνούς δικτύου. The -ων / -ουσα / -ον Participles: These are ancient active participles that act like adjectives (similar to the English "-ing"). ενδιαφέρων (Masc), ενδιαφέρουσα (Fem), ενδιαφέρον (Neut) = Interesting. υπάρχων (existing), μέλλων (future). 🎯 Unit 31 Mastery Checklist I understand that Modern Greek contains formal remnants from Katharevousa and Ancient Greek. I have memorized common frozen Dative expressions like Εν τω μεταξύ . I know that words like Λόγω and Κατά are formal prepositions that take the Genitive case. I recognize the formal feminine Genitive ending -εως and the mandatory plural -εις / -εων . I can recognize learned C2 adjectives ending in -ης and participles like ενδιαφέρων . Practice Vocabulary: C2 Vocabulary: Ancient Phrases for Formal Greek Up Next: Unit 32: Genitive Absolute & Ancient Participles

aorist-past.title: Unit 14: The Past Tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist)
aorist-past.content: Unit 14: The Past Tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist) Welcome to the capstone of the A2 Expansion stage. You’ve learned how to build stems and how to talk about the future. Now, we look back. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the Imperfect vs. Aorist, the "Rule of 3" for the augment (ε-), and the irregular past forms like "eida" and "eipa". Just like the future, Greek forces you to choose between two ways to talk about the past: the Video and the Photo . TIP "Aorist" vs. "Simple Past" While traditional textbooks use the ancient term "Aorist", modern grammarians prefer the term "Simple Past" . In this course, we will use both terms interchangeably to describe a single, completed past event. 1. The Two Past Tenses The Imperfect (The Video): Used for habits, routines, or actions that were ongoing in the past (e.g., "I was playing ", "I used to play "). Uses the Continuous (Present) Stem. The Aorist (The Photo): Used for a single event completed at a specific point in the past (e.g., "I played "). Uses the Simple (Perfective) Stem. 2. Past Tense Endings & The "Rule of 3" Both past tenses (Imperfect and Aorist) share the exact same set of past-tense endings! The only difference is which stem you stick them onto. Person Past Ending Example (Aorist of γράφω) Translation Εγώ -α έ -γραψ- α I wrote Εσύ -ες έ -γραψ- ες You wrote Αυτός/ή/ό -ε έ -γραψ- ε He/She/It wrote Εμείς -αμε γράψ- αμε We wrote Εσείς -ατε γράψ- ατε You wrote Αυτοί/ές/ά -αν(ε) έ -γραψ- αν They wrote IMPORTANT The Augment (έ-) & The Rule of 3 Past tense verbs in Greek have a strict rhythm: the stress must fall on the third syllable from the end. What happens if the word is too short? For example, the stem γραψ- plus the ending -α is only two syllables ( γρά-ψα ). To fix this, Greek glues an έ- to the front of the word so it has a third syllable to stress: έ-γρα-ψα . This is called the Augment . Notice how it magically disappears in the plural forms ( γρά-ψα-με ) because the word is finally long enough! NOTE Vocalic Augments (ή-) If a verb already starts with a vowel (like ε λπίζω - to hope), it doesn't add an έ- . Instead, the starting vowel stretches into an ή- (e.g., ή λπιζα - I was hoping). 3. Side-by-Side: Video vs. Photo Let's use the verb παίζω (I play). Continuous Stem: παίζ- Simple Stem: παίξ- A. The Imperfect (Continuous Stem) Έπαιζα τένις κάθε μέρα. (I used to play tennis every day - Habit). Έπαιζα όταν χτύπησε το τηλέφωνο. (I was playing when the phone rang - Background action). B. The Aorist (Simple Stem) Έπαιξα τένις χθες. (I played tennis yesterday — once, completed). Το Σάββατο έπαιξα καλά. (On Saturday I played well). 4. The absolute Irregulars These are the most common verbs in the past. You must memorize them immediately. Notice how their stems completely change shapeshift! βλέπω (see) ➔ είδα (I saw) λέω (say) ➔ είπα (I said) πηγαίνω / πάω (go) ➔ πήγα (I went) βρίσκω (find) ➔ βρήκα (I found) έρχομαι (come) ➔ ήρθα (I came) The "Defective" Verbs (No Photo Allowed!) The two most important verbs in Greek, έχω (have) and είμαι (be), are physically unable to form an Aorist (Photo) tense. They only exist as continuous states! έχω ➔ είχα (I had) είμαι ➔ ήμουν (α) (I was) (You cannot say "I was" or "I had" as a single, momentary action in Greek). 🎯 A2 Expansion Capstone Congratulations! You have completed the A2 Stage . You have mastered: Pluralization of all nouns and adjectives. The "Big Two" Verb Groups (B1 & B2). Verbal Aspect (Video vs. Photo) and the Subjunctive ( να ). Both Future tenses and the mechanics of the Simple Stem. Both Past tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist) and the "Rule of 3" for the Augment. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & B-Group Verbs Next module (B1 Intermediate): Unit 15: The Genitive Case (Possession)

basics.title: Unit 1: The Greek Writing System Masterclass
basics.content: Unit 1: The Greek Writing System Masterclass Welcome to your first step toward mastering Greek. Forget everything you've heard about Greek being "impossible"—as an English speaker, learning the Greek alphabet is an incredibly satisfying and fast process. Over 25% of the English vocabulary has Greek roots; you already know more Greek than you realize. This comprehensive guide is designed to take you from zero to reading Modern Greek fluently. We will break the alphabet down into manageable, logical groups. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's core concepts explained with native phonetic references. 1. The 24 Letters The Greek alphabet consists of 24 letters. Instead of learning them in alphabetical order, let's group them by how they relate to the English alphabet. This is the fastest way to bridge the gap. Group 1: The Twins These letters are your best friends. They look and sound almost exactly like their English counterparts. Letter Name Sound Example (in English) Α α Alpha [a] f a ther Ε ε Epsilon [e] p e t Ι ι Iota [ee] m ee t Κ κ Kappa [k] k ite Μ μ Mu [m] m om Ν ν Nu [n] n o Ο ο Omicron [o] n o t Τ τ Tau [t] t op Ζ ζ Zeta [z] z oo TIP The Crisp Pronunciation Rule: In English, we often release a puff of air when saying consonants like P, T, and K (this is called aspiration ). In Greek, π [p], τ [t], and κ [k] are perfectly crisp. Say "spot" instead of "pot"—that crisp 'p' is exactly how Greek sounds. Group 2: The False Friends These letters look familiar, but they are trying to trick you! Pay close attention to these, as they require rewiring your brain slightly. Letter Name Sound Example (in English) Β β Beta [v] v an (It is NEVER a 'b') Η η Eta [ee] m ee t (It is NEVER an 'h') Ρ ρ Rho [r] a lightly rolled r (Never a 'p') Χ χ Chi [h] Scottish lo ch or human h eat Υ υ Upsilon [ee] m ee t (Never a 'y' or 'u') Group 3: The True Greeks These are the iconic, unique shapes of the Greek alphabet. You've probably seen them in physics, math, or fraternities. Letter Name Sound Example (in English) Γ γ Gamma [gh] / [y] y es (soft before vowels e/i) Δ δ Delta [th] th is (voiced, soft) Θ θ Theta [th] th ink (voiceless, hard) Λ λ Lambda [l] l ion Ξ ξ Xi [ks] bo x Π π Pi [p] p en Σ σ, ς Sigma [s] s un (becomes ς only at the end of a word) Φ φ Phi [f] f ish Ψ ψ Psi [ps] li ps Ω ω Omega [o] n o t (exact same sound as Ο ο) NOTE The Secret of Sigma: Sigma has a special rule. When it's in the middle of a word, it looks like σ (e.g., θάλασ σ α - sea). But if it is the very last letter of a word, it drops its tail and becomes ς (e.g., κόσμο ς - world). Either way, it sounds exactly the same! 2. The Digraphs (Sound Combinations) Sometimes, Greek glues two letters together to create an entirely new sound. Think of English "sh" or "th". The Vowel Combinations (Diphthongs) Greek loves the "ee" sound. In fact, there are six different ways to make it! αι = [e] like in p e t (e.g., αι τία - cause) ει, οι, υι = [ee] like in m ee t (e.g., εί μαι - I am) ου = [oo] like in b oo t (e.g., ου ρανός - sky) IMPORTANT The Chameleon Vowels (αυ & ευ): When α or ε team up with υ , the υ transforms from a vowel into a consonant! αυ / ευ sound like [af] / [ef] when followed by unvoiced consonants (like κ, π, τ). αυ / ευ sound like [av] / [ev] when followed by vowels or voiced consonants (like γ, δ, β). The Consonant Combinations Over thousands of years, ancient Greek sounds evolved. To write modern sounds like B, D, and G, Greeks had to get creative by combining existing letters. μπ = B (e.g., μπ α μπ άς - baba, dad) ντ = D (e.g., ντ ύνω - dino, to dress) γκ / γγ = G (e.g., ά γκ υρα - agira, anchor) τσ = TS (e.g., τσ έπη - tsepi, pocket) τζ = J / DZ (e.g., τζ άκι - jaki, fireplace) 3. Accents & Punctuation Modern Greek is remarkably phonetic. Once you know the rules above, you can confidently pronounce any word you see. A huge part of this is due to the accent mark. The Tonos (´) Every Greek word with two or more syllables carries a tiny mark called the tonos over one vowel. This tells you exactly which syllable to stress. νερό (ne-RÓ) - water πρόβλημα (PRÓ-vli-ma) - problem If you see a tonos over a vowel combination (like εί ), the sound doesn't change, but that's where you put your vocal energy. Punctuation Marks Greek punctuation is almost identical to English, with two brilliant exceptions: 1. The Question Mark (;) : The Greek question mark looks exactly like an English semicolon! (e.g., Τι κάνεις; = How are you?) 2. The Upper Dot (·) : This acts like an English semicolon or colon, signaling a pause longer than a comma but shorter than a period. 🎯 Unit 1 Mastery Checklist I can accurately identify the 24 letters and their sounds. I can distinguish the "False Friends" (like Β, Η, Ρ, Χ, Υ) from their English look-alikes. I know the six ways to write the [ee] sound (ι, η, υ, ει, οι, υι). I understand how μπ, ντ, and γκ create the B, D, and G sounds. I know what the tonos (´) does and why it is crucial for pronunciation. I recognize that ; means "question" in Greek. Ready to practice? A1 Vocabulary: Survival Phrases Next Lesson: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns & Articles

compound-words.title: Unit 30: Compound Words & Prefixation
compound-words.content: Unit 30: Compound Words & Prefixation Greek vocabulary is massive, but it's incredibly logical. Throughout its long history, Greek has always had a great facility for creating new words. Instead of inventing brand new words for every concept, Greeks build words like Lego bricks by modifying stems with prefixes, or snapping two smaller words together. Once you understand how the bricks connect, you can usually guess the meaning of a C1/C2 word even if you've never seen it before! TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the Greek "glue" (o), the stress shift for the Alpha Privative, and reversing actions with "Ξε-". 1. The Greek "Glue": The letter Omikron (o) When you attach two Greek words together to form a compound, you almost always need "glue" to connect them. The elements that make up a compound are typically linked by the vowel -ο- . If the first word ends in a normal vowel (like -ος, -η, or -ι), that vowel simply turns into an ο . Then the second word attaches to it! βιβλίο (book) + πωλώ (I sell) ➔ βιβλιοπωλείο (bookstore). μαχαίρι (knife) + πιρούνι (fork) ➔ μαχαιροπίρουνο (knife and fork). Σάββατο (Saturday) + Κυριακή (Sunday) ➔ Σαββατοκύριακο (weekend). 2. Prefixes (The Power Bricks) There are dozens of highly active prefixes (mini-words) that you can slap onto the front of any word to instantly change its meaning. Α- / Αν- (The Negative / Un-) This is the famous Alpha Privative. Greek uses a simple α- (if the next letter is a consonant) or αν- (if the next letter is a vowel). IMPORTANT C1 Pronunciation Secret: When adding the alpha privative to form a negative adjective, the stress normally shifts to the third syllable from the end ! γνωστός (known) ➔ άγνωστος (unknown). φυσικός (natural) ➔ αφύσικος (unnatural). πιθανός (probable) ➔ απίθανος (improbable / amazing). Ξε- (The Reverser / Un-) Prefixed to verbs to reverse the action (like tying and un-tying). κλειδώνω (I lock) ➔ ξεκλειδώνω (I unlock). ντύνω (I dress) ➔ ξεντύνω (I undress). διψώ (I am thirsty) ➔ ξεδιψώ (I quench my thirst). Ξανα- (Again / Re-) If you want to say "do it again" or "re-do", just add ξανα- to the verb! κάνω (I do) ➔ ξανακάνω (I re-do). διαβάζω (I read) ➔ ξαναδιαβάζω (I reread). TIP Bonus C1 Prefix: Κατα- (Very much) You can stick κατα- in front of adjectives to mean "extremely", or verbs to intensify the action. άσπρος (white) ➔ κάτασπρος (very white). βρέχω (I wet) ➔ καταβρέχω (I soak / drench). 3. Advanced Combinations You can stick almost any parts of speech together to create powerful descriptive words. Always remember to use the glue ο ! Adjective + Adjective: ασπρόμαυρος (black and white). Adjective + Noun: κοκκινομάλλης (red-haired), στενόμυαλος (narrow-minded). Adjective/Adverb + Participle: φρεσκοβαμμένος (freshly painted), μαυροντυμένος (dressed in black). 🎭 The Action Combos (Verb + Verb) Greek frequently creates compounds by sticking two verbs together, usually connected with the ο glue. ανοίγω (open) + κλείνω (close) ➔ ανοιγοκλείνω (I open and close). πηγαίνω (go) + έρχομαι (come) ➔ πηγαινοέρχομαι (I come and go / I pacing). ανεβαίνω (go up) + κατεβαίνω (go down) ➔ ανεβοκατεβαίνω (I go up and down). 4. Famous Greek Roots Once you hit C1, you will see these roots everywhere. -φίλος (Lover / Friend): ζωόφιλος (animal lover), φιλόξενος (hospitable). -φόβος (Fearer / Hater): ξενοφοβία (fear of foreigners), υψοφοβία (fear of heights). -λόγος (Speaker / Student of): ψυχολόγος (psychologist). -γράφος (Writer): μυθιστοριογράφος (novelist). 🎯 Unit 30 Mastery Checklist I know that Greek builds words using the letter ο as "glue". I understand the stress shift rule for the Alpha Privative ( α- / αν- ). I can reverse an action using ξε- and repeat it using ξανα- . I can identify and use advanced combinations like ανοιγοκλείνω . Practice Vocabulary: C1 Vocabulary: Compound Words in Everyday Life Up Next: Unit 31: Ancient Remnants & Formal Registers

conditionals-1-2.title: Unit 21: Conditionals (The 'If' Clauses)
conditionals-1-2.content: Unit 21: Conditionals (The 'If' Clauses) Welcome to the end of the B1 Intermediate stage. You are about to unlock one of the most powerful structures in any language: The Conditional sentence ("If X, then Y") . In Greek grammar, the "If" half of the sentence is called the Protasis , and the "Then" half is the Apodosis . To master these, you just need to balance the tenses on both sides of the comma. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the three types of conditionals, the difference between "Αν" and "Άμα", and the shortcut natives use for past regrets. TIP How to say "If" The standard and most common word for "If" is Αν . However, in spoken Greek, you will constantly hear άμα (which means "if" or "whenever"). In formal writing, you will see εάν . 1. Type 1: The Real World (Highly Likely) Use this when talking about facts, plans, warnings, or things that possess a real possibility of happening. The Balance: [Αν + Present or Future] ➔ [Future or Command] Αν βρέχει , δεν θα πάμε παραλία. (If it rains, we will not go to the beach). Άμα τελειώσεις νωρίς, πάρε με τηλέφωνο. (If you finish early, call me). Αν πεινάς , υπάρχει φαγητό στο ψυγείο. (If you are hungry, there is food in the fridge). CAUTION Negating the "If" How do you say "If you don't ..."? Even though "If" statements feel hypothetical, Greek grammar treats them as solid facts. Therefore, clauses starting with Αν must be negated by the strong particle δεν (never μην !). Αν δεν θέλεις... (If you don't want to...) 2. Type 2: The Unreal (Dreams & Hypotheticals) Use this to talk about present dreams, giving advice to someone else, or things that are highly unlikely or impossible right now. To create distance from reality, Greek pushes both verbs one step backward into the continuous past tense! The Balance: [Αν + Imperfect Past] ➔ [ Θα + Imperfect Past] Αν είχα λεφτά, θα αγόραζα ένα νησί. (If I had money [now] , I would buy an island). Αν ήμουν στη θέση σου, δεν θα πήγαινα . (If I were in your place, I wouldn't go). Άμα δούλευε λιγότερο, θα ήταν πιο χαρούμενος. (If he worked less, he would be happier). (Notice how Θα + Past Tense is the Greek way of saying "Would"!). 3. Type 3: The Regret (Too Late) Use this for past regrets—things that never happened, and it is too late to change them. You must push the verbs even further back, using the Past Perfect (Pluperfect) from Unit 20. The Balance: [Αν + Past Perfect] ➔ [ Θα + Past Perfect] Αν είχα διαβάσει περισσότερο, θα είχα περάσει τις εξετάσεις. (If I had studied more, I would have passed the exams). Αν δεν είχε βρέξει , θα είχαμε πάει βόλτα. (If it hadn't rained, we would have gone for a walk). TIP The Native Shortcut The Type 3 "Regret" structure is very long and heavy. In casual street Greek, native speakers often take a shortcut and just use the shorter Type 2 structure to complain about the past! Formal: Αν είχες έρθει νωρίτερα, θα τον είχες δει . (If you had come earlier, you would have seen him.) Street: Αν ερχόσουν νωρίτερα, θα τον έβλεπες . (If you came earlier, you would see him.) The context makes it obvious they are talking about yesterday! 🎯 B1 Intermediate Capstone Congratulations! You have mastered the B1 Stage . You now possess the power to: Express ownership perfectly using the Genitive case. Give positive, continuous, and negative commands (Imperative). Use adverbs gracefully. Maneuver through Deponent and Passive verbs. Master the Perfect Tenses (I have/had done). Construct complex "If / Then" conditional sentences. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Travel & Life Experiences Next module (C1 Advanced): Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Spoken Interjections

deponent-verbs.title: Unit 18: Passive Voice & Deponent Verbs (-ομαι)
deponent-verbs.content: Unit 18: Passive Voice & Deponent Verbs (-ομαι) Welcome to the other half of the Greek verb universe: Type C Verbs . These verbs do not end in -ω . They end in -ομαι (o-me). This special ending system is used for two very different things in Greek: Deponent Verbs and the Passive Voice . TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on Deponent vs. Passive verbs, the first person plural accent shift, and the Past Passive suffixes (-στηκα, -φτηκα, -χτηκα). 1. What are Deponent Verbs? Some of the most common verbs in Greek wear a disguise. They look exactly like passive verbs (they end in -ομαι), but their meaning is completely active . We call these Deponent Verbs . They only exist in this form—you can never say "έρχω" or "χρειάζω". έρχομαι (I come) ➔ Από πού έρχεσαι; (Where are you coming from?) χρειάζομαι (I need) ➔ Χρειάζομαι λίγο χρόνο. (I need a little time). σκέφτομαι (I think) ➔ Τι σκέφτεσαι; (What are you thinking about?) φοβάμαι (I fear / I am afraid) CAUTION The "Semi-Deponents" Some incredibly common verbs are "semi-deponent". They look passive in the Present tense, but when you put them into the Simple Past (the Photo), they drop their disguise and take normal Active endings! γίνομαι (I become) ➔ Past: έγινα (I became - Active Ending! ). έρχομαι (I come) ➔ Past: ήρθα (I came - Active Ending! ). κάθομαι (I sit) ➔ Past: κάθισα (I sat - Active Ending! ). 2. Conjugating Type C (Present Tense) Whether a verb is Deponent or True Passive, it uses the same ending system. Person Ending Example: έρχομαι (come) (Εγώ) -ομαι έρχ ομαι (Εσύ) -εσαι έρχ εσαι (Αυτό/ή/ό) -εται έρχ εται (Εμείς) -όμαστε ερχ όμαστε (Εσείς) -εστε / -όσαστε έρχ εστε / ερχ όσαστε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ονται έρχ ονται TIP The Accent Shift & Street Smart Endings In the "We" form ( ερχόμαστε ), the accent mark is forced to jump one syllable to the right. Also, for the "You all" form, -εστε is the standard. However, you will constantly hear Greeks use the colloquial alternative -όσαστε (which also has an accent shift!). Both are perfectly correct. 3. The True Passive Voice & Reflexives You can also take a normal active verb (like πλένω - I wash) and deliberately flip it into a Type C form ( πλένομαι - I am washed). In Greek, the passive voice is very often used reflexively (doing something to yourself). Active: Πλένω το αυτοκίνητο. (I wash the car). Reflexive: Πλένομαι . (I wash myself / I am taking a bath). Active: Ξυρίζει τον πελάτη. (He shaves the customer). Reflexive: Ξυρίζομαι . (I shave myself). "By Someone" (The Agent) If you want to say who performed the action in a passive sentence, use the preposition από + Accusative case. The object of the active sentence becomes the new subject! Active: Ο Γιώργος διαβάζει το βιβλίο . (George reads the book). Passive: Το βιβλίο διαβάζεται από τον Γιώργο. (The book is read by George). 4. The Past Passive (Aorist) To put a passive or reflexive verb into the Simple Past (The Photo), you use a new set of endings characterized by the syllable -ηκ- . The stress stays fiercely on the third syllable from the end. Person Ending Example: πλένομαι ➔ πλύθηκα (Εγώ) -ηκα πλύθ ηκα (I was washed) (Εσύ) -ηκες πλύθ ηκες (Αυτός/ή/ό) -ηκε πλύθ ηκε (Εμείς) -ήκαμε πλυθ ήκαμε (Εσείς) -ήκατε πλυθ ήκατε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ηκαν / -ήκανε πλύθ ηκαν WARNING The Stem Cheat Codes Return! Do you remember the simple stem groups (Σ, Ξ, Ψ) from Unit 12? They control the consonant right before the -ηκα ending! The 'S' Group (dental/nasal/vocalic): Uses -στηκα or -θηκα . (ετοιμάζω ➔ ετοιμά στ ηκα: I got ready). The 'PS' (Lip) Group: Uses -φτηκα . (κρύβω ➔ κρύ φτ ηκα: I hid myself). The 'X' (Throat) Group: Uses -χτηκα . (ανοίγω ➔ ανοί χτ ηκα: I opened myself up / I was opened). 🎯 Unit 18 Mastery Checklist I can conjugate έρχομαι and remember the accent shift in ερχόμαστε . I know that Deponent verbs look passive but have an active meaning. I know what a "semi-deponent" verb is ( έρχομαι ➔ ήρθα ). I can flip an active verb into a reflexive one ( πλένω ➔ πλένομαι ). I can use από + Accusative to express "by someone" in a passive sentence. I recognize the -ηκα, -στηκα, -φτηκα, -χτηκα endings as Past Passive markers. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Deponent Verbs & States Next Lesson: Unit 19: Voices, Moods & Special Forms

diminutives.title: Unit 29: Diminutives & Augmentatives (Υποκοριστικά & Μεγεθυντικά)
diminutives.content: Diminutives & Augmentatives (Υποκοριστικά & Μεγεθυντικά) Greek is a language of intense emotion. You don't just drink a coffee; you drink a little coffee with a friend. You don't just see a dog; you see a huge, monstrous dog. To express these feelings of smallness, affection, or enormous size, Greek uses special suffixes attached to the stem of nouns. Mastering these suffixes is the key to sounding like a true native speaker! TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the universal "-άκι", gender-specific diminutives, and the augmentative "-αρος" used to make things sound completely massive! 1. The Diminutives (Making things small, cute, or polite) Diminutives express small size, familiarity, affection, or sometimes even depreciation. They are incredibly common in spoken Greek. When you order food, you rarely order a "τυρόπιτα" (cheese pie). You order a "τυροπιτάκι" (little cheese pie), because it sounds friendlier! Suffix 1: -άκι (The Universal Tiny-Maker) This is by far the most common diminutive. It can be added to almost any noun, and it always turns the word into a Neuter noun (Το). το σπίτι (the house) ➔ το σπιτάκι (the little house). το παιδί (the child) ➔ το παιδάκι (the small child). ο καφές (the coffee) ➔ το καφεδάκι (the small coffee). ο μεζές (the appetizer) ➔ το μεζεδάκι (the snack). (Notice how even masculine nouns become neuter when you add -άκι!) Suffix 2: -ίτσα & -ούλα (For Feminine Nouns) If you want to keep a word feminine while making it cute, you can use these suffixes attached to the stem. η μπύρα (the beer) ➔ η μπιρίτσα (the little beer). η ώρα (the hour) ➔ η ωρίτσα (a brief hour or so). η αδερφή (the sister) ➔ η αδερφούλα (the little sister). η βάρκα (the boat) ➔ η βαρκούλα (the little boat). (These are also very common for female names: η Ελένη ➔ η Ελενίτσα ). Suffix 3: -ούλης & -άκης (For Masculine Nouns) If you want to make a masculine person sound cute or endearing. ο πατέρας (the father) ➔ ο πατερούλης (daddy). ο κόσμος (the world/people) ➔ ο κοσμάκης (the ordinary people). (The -άκης suffix is also extremely common for male proper names, like Μανωλάκης). TIP C1 Advanced Tip: Diminutive Adjectives! You can also make adjectives cute or "moderate"! Greek uses the suffix -ούτσικος to mean "to a moderate extent" (like the English "-ish"). καλός (good) ➔ καλούτσικος (quite good). μεγάλος (big) ➔ μεγαλούτσικος (biggish). ξινό (sour) ➔ ξινούτσικο (a little sour). 2. The Augmentatives (Making things HUGE) Sometimes you need to emphasize that something is massive, impressive, or even a bit scary. Augmentatives express large size or admiration. Suffix 1: -αρος (The Giant) This is the most common masculine augmentative. το σκυλί (the dog) ➔ ο σκύλαρος (the great big dog). το παιδί (the boy/lad) ➔ ο παίδαρος (the big, handsome lad). Suffix 2: -άρα & -α (The Huge Feminine) This creates a sense of enormous, sometimes overwhelming size. το αυτοκίνητο (the car) ➔ η αυτοκινητάρα (the huge, impressive car). η κοιλιά (the belly) ➔ η κοιλάρα (the big belly). το μπουκάλι (the bottle) ➔ η μπουκάλα (the big bottle). IMPORTANT Native Secret: The "Possessor of a Quality" (-αράς / -ακλάς) If you want to describe a man who has a HUGE amount of a certain quality, use -αράς or -ακλάς . η δουλειά (work) ➔ ο δουλευταράς (the incredibly hard worker). η φωνή (voice) ➔ ο φωνακλάς (the loud-mouth). 3. The Pragmatic Power of Emotion Remember, these suffixes aren't just about physical size. Greek often uses the diminutive forms of nouns and adjectives with acts like offers, compliments, and requests as a device to show affection or to minimize the imposition placed by the speaker on the addressee. If a friend invites you to their house and says, "Πάρτε ένα μπισκοτάκι" (Take a little biscuit), they are using the diminutive to make the offer sound polite and unobtrusive. When you ask a waiter, "Μας φέρνετε μια μπιρίτσα;" (Can you bring us a little beer?), it sounds incredibly friendly and casual. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist I know that -άκι makes words small/cute, and always turns them into Το (Neuter). I know the feminine diminutives -ίτσα and -ούλα . I know the masculine diminutives -ούλης and -άκης . I can use -ούτσικος to say an adjective is "moderate" or "-ish". I know that -αρος, -άρα, and -αράς are used to make things sound completely massive! Practice Vocabulary: C1 Vocabulary: Nuance & Emotion Up Next: Unit 30: Compound Words

exercise-actions.title: Unit 4-5: Accusative Action
exercise-actions.content: Unit 4-5: Action & The Accusative This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (A rainy street in Thessaloniki, Greece. People holding umbrellas and hurrying) Power Lexis: Greek Pronunciation English Η βροχή (i vro-chí) The rain Η ομπρέλα (i om-bré-la) The umbrella Κρατάω (kra-táo) I hold Τρέχω (tré-kho) I run Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Use one Type A action verb (e.g. κρατάω, τρέχω). 2. Include a direct object in the Accusative case . Pro Tip: Remember that feminine nouns keep the "α" or "η" in the accusative, but the definite article changes from "η" to "την". 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Βρέχει πολύ σήμερα. Ο άνθρωπος κρατάει την ομπρέλα.» (It is raining a lot today. The man is holding the umbrella.) Why it works: κρατάει την ομπρέλα : "Κρατάει" is the 3rd person singular of the Type A action verb "κρατάω". "Την ομπρέλα" is the direct object receiving the action, correctly inflected in the accusative case (changing from "Η ομπρέλα" to "Την ομπρέλα"). 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base Sentence Trigger Target Sentence Διαβάζω το βιβλίο. (I read the book.) Change subject to Ο δάσκαλος Ο δάσκαλος διαβάζει το βιβλίο. Κρατάει την ομπρέλα. (He/She holds the umbrella.) Change subject to Εγώ (I) Κρατάω την ομπρέλα. Τρέχω γρήγορα. (I run fast.) Change subject to Η γυναίκα (The woman) Η γυναίκα τρέχει γρήγορα. Βλέπει τον καφέ. (He/She sees the coffee.) Change subject to Εμείς (We) Βλέπουμε τον καφέ. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of the Accusative Case and Type A verbs. «Βλέπω την τσάντα. Η γυναίκα κρατάει την τσάντα. Βλέπω τον καφέ. Ο άντρας πίνει τον καφέ. Εγώ έχω το νερό. Πίνω το νερό και βλέπω τον ουρανό. Κάνω μία βόλτα.»

exercise-adjectives.title: Unit 6-7: Adjectives & Objects
exercise-adjectives.content: Unit 6-7: Adjectives & Questions This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (Two people looking at colorful fresh fruit at an outdoor Greek market under the sunlight) Power Lexis: Greek Pronunciation English Φρέσκος, -η, -ο (frés-kos) Fresh Κόκκινος, -η, -ο (kó-ki-nos) Red Πόσο (pó-so) How much Κάνει (ká-ni) It costs (literally "it makes") Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Ask a question using the essential phrase Πόσο κάνει; 2. Use an adjective agreeing in gender with its noun. Pro Tip: In Greek, adjectives must always match the gender (masculine, feminine, or neuter), number, and case of the noun they describe. 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Αυτό το φρούτο είναι κόκκινο και φρέσκο. Πόσο κάνει;» (This fruit is red and fresh. How much does it cost?) Why it works: κόκκινο και φρέσκο : The noun "το φρούτο" is neuter. Therefore, the adjectives "κόκκινος" and "φρέσκος" must drop their masculine "-ος" ending and take the neuter "-ο" ending to become "κόκκινο" and "φρέσκο". Πόσο κάνει; : This is the standard, everyday way to ask for the price of a singular item in Greece. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base Sentence Trigger Target Sentence Το κόκκινο μήλο κάνει δύο ευρώ. (The red apple costs two euros.) Change subject to Ο καφές (Masculine) Ο κόκκινος καφές κάνει δύο ευρώ. Πόσο κάνει η μικρή τσάντα; (How much does the small bag cost?) Change subject to Το βιβλίο (Neuter) Πόσο κάνει το μικρό βιβλίο; Το φρέσκο νερό είναι κρύο. (The fresh water is cold.) Change subject to Η σαλάτα (Feminine) Η φρέσκια σαλάτα είναι κρύα. Έχω ένα μεγάλο πρόβλημα. (I have a big problem - Neuter) Change object to Η ιδέα (Feminine) Έχω μία μεγάλη ιδέα. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of adjectives and basic questions. «Το μεγάλο σπίτι έχει μία κόκκινη πόρτα. Η μικρή πόρτα είναι ανοιχτή. Ποιος είναι εκεί; Ένας καλός φίλος είναι εκεί. Τι θέλει; Θέλει λίγο κρύο νερό. Πόσο κάνει το κρύο νερό; Είναι δωρεάν!»

exercise-basics.title: Unit 1-3: The Basics
exercise-basics.content: Unit 1-3: Foundations This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (An old Greek grandmother cooking in a rustic, sunlight-filled kitchen) Power Lexis: Greek Pronunciation English Η γιαγιά (i ya-yá) The grandmother Το φαγητό (to fa-yi-tó) The food Είναι (í-ne) He/She/It is Έχω (é-kho) I have Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Describe the scene using the verb "to be" (είμαι). 2. Describe an action/possession using the verb "to have" (έχω). Pro Tip: Keep it simple! Focus on creating short, complete sentences that use the definite article correctly. 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Έχει ωραίο φαγητό.» (The grandmother is in the kitchen. She has nice food.) Why it works: Η γιαγιά είναι... : Correct use of the feminine definite article "η" with the subject noun and the 3rd person singular present tense form of "είμαι". Έχει ωραίο φαγητό : The verb "έχω" is conjugated to the 3rd person singular ("έχει" - she has). "Ωραίο φαγητό" (nice food) follows as the related object of possession. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base Sentence Trigger Target Sentence Εγώ είμαι στην κουζίνα. (I am in the kitchen.) Change subject to Η γιαγιά Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Έχω ωραίο φαγητό. (I have nice food.) Change subject to Ο πατέρας Ο πατέρας έχει ωραίο φαγητό. Ο φίλος είναι εδώ. (The friend is here.) Change subject to Εμείς (We) Εμείς είμαστε εδώ. Η μητέρα έχει ένα βιβλίο. (The mother has a book.) Change subject to Εσύ (You) Εσύ έχεις ένα βιβλίο. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of to be and to have . «Το σπίτι είναι μεγάλο. Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Έχει ωραίο φαγητό. Το παιδί είναι εδώ. Έχει ένα βιβλίο. Το βιβλίο είναι καλό. Η μητέρα είναι και αυτή εδώ. Είναι όλοι καλά!»

exercise-plurals.title: Unit 8-10: Plurals & Verbs
exercise-plurals.content: Unit 8-10: Plurals & Verb Groups This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (A stylish woman trying on different coats in a high-end boutique in Athens) Power Lexis: Greek Pronunciation English Το παλτό -> Τα παλτά (to pal-tó) -> (ta pal-tá) The coat Μικρός, -ή, -ό -> Μικρά (mi-krós) -> (mi-krá) Small Φοράω (fo-ráo) I wear Ακριβός, -ή, -ό (a-kri-vós) Expensive Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Use Plural Nouns and Adjectives . 2. Conjgate a Group B verb (e.g. φοράω). Pro Tip: Neuter nouns ending in -ο or stressed -ό in the singular change to -α or stressed -ά in the plural. Adjectives follow the exact same pattern! 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Η γυναίκα φοράει τα μικρά παλτά. Είναι πολύ ακριβά!» (The woman is wearing the small coats. They are very expensive!) Why it works: τα μικρά παλτά / πολύ ακριβά : "Το παλτό" becomes "Τα παλτά" in the plural. Accordingly, the adjectives "μικρό" and "ακριβό" change their endings to match the neuter plural form (μικρά, ακριβά). φοράει : "Φοράω" belongs to Verb Group B1 (verbs ending in an unstressed -άω in the "I" form). The "she" form takes the -άει ending. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base Sentence Trigger Target Sentence Η γυναίκα φοράει το παλτό. (The woman wears the coat.) Change object to Plural Η γυναίκα φοράει τα παλτά. Το μικρό παιδί παίζει. (The small child plays.) Change subject to Plural Τα μικρά παιδιά παίζουν. Αγοράζω το ακριβό ρούχο. (I buy the expensive piece of clothing.) Change object to Plural Αγοράζω τα ακριβά ρούχα. Ο πωλητής μιλάει πολύ. (The salesperson talks a lot.) Change subject to Plural Οι πωλητές μιλούν πολύ. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of Plural Nouns and Group B Verbs. «Τα μαγαζιά είναι ακριβά. Οι γυναίκες αγαπούν τα ωραία ρούχα. Δοκιμάζουν τα μικρά φορέματα και τα μεγάλα παλτά. Οι πωλητές μιλούν πολύ. Τα ρούχα κοστίζουν πολλά ευρώ. Όλοι γελάνε.»

future-continuous.title: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα) & Aspect
future-continuous.content: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα) & Aspect In Unit 10, you learned the magic of Aspect (The Video vs. The Photo). Now, we are going to apply that exact same logic to the future. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the Future particle "Θα", Continuous vs. Simple Future, and the "Pronoun Sandwich" rule. In English, we just say "I will play." Greek demands more precision: are you going to play regularly as a habit (a Video), or are you talking about one specific game tomorrow (a Photo)? 1. The Future Particle: Θα To talk about the future in Greek, you don't learn a whole new set of verb endings. Instead, you just use the tiny magic particle θα (which means "will") and place it right in front of the verb! Παίζω (I play) ➔ Θα παίζω (I will be playing). IMPORTANT The Pronoun Sandwich Θα loves being right next to the verb. The ONLY things allowed to come between θα and the verb are short object pronouns (like "me", "it", "him"). When this happens, they form a little sandwich! Θα το κάνω. (I will do it ). Θα σου το δώσω. (I will give it to you ). 2. The Continuous Future (The "Video") Use this future tense when you want to talk about a habit, a routine, or an action that will be ongoing in the future. The Formula: Θα + Present Tense Verb (The Continuous, "Video" Stem) Θα διαβάζω κάθε μέρα. (I will be reading every day). Κάθε Τετάρτη θα τρώμε μπέργκερ. (Every Wednesday we will eat burgers). 3. The Simple Future (The "Photo") Use this future tense for a single, specific event that will happen and be completed at a particular point in time. This is by far the most common way to talk about the future! The Formula: Θα + The Simple ("Photo") Stem Θα διαβάσω το βιβλίο αύριο. (I will read the book tomorrow—just once). Θα φάμε μια πίτσα απόψε. (We will eat a pizza tonight). CAUTION The Augment Trap We will learn about the Past Tense in a few units, but here is a massive warning: Many students try to build the Simple Future by borrowing the Simple Past exact form. But the Past Tense often has an extra "ε-" at the front (called an augment). You must NEVER use the past tense 'ε-' in the future! It is strictly a time machine for the past. Past: έ γραψα (I wrote). Future: θα γράψω (I will write — The 'ε' is stripped away! ). 4. How to Say "I won't" (Negation) Remember the golden rule from Unit 10? Verbs after να take μη(ν) . But θα is different! To negate a future sentence, you use your old friend δεν , placing it right before θα . Δεν θα παίζω ποδόσφαιρο πια. (I won't be playing football anymore - habit ). Δεν θα παίξω αύριο. (I won't play tomorrow - single event ). Δεν θα σου το δώσω. (I won't give it to you). 5. Speak Like a Native: The Probability Trick Greeks don't just use θα for the future. They frequently use it to express probability, guessing, or supposing something right now! It works exactly like the English "must be" or "must have". Somebody knocks on the door: Θα είναι ο Γιώργος. (It must be George). They are late: Θα έφυγαν. (They must have left ). 🎯 Unit 11 Mastery Checklist I can choose between θα παίζω (Continuous/habit) and θα παίξω (Simple/one-off event). I know how to negate the future using δεν θα . I know to sandwich short object pronouns between θα and the verb ( θα το κάνω ). I understand that the past tense augment (like έ- ) is banned from the future tense. I know how to use θα to guess that someone "must be" at the door. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Time & Calendar Next Lesson: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem

genitive-absolute.title: Unit 32: Participles in the Genitive Absolute (Γενική Απόλυτη)
genitive-absolute.content: Participles in the Genitive Absolute (Γενική Απόλυτη) In Level B2, you learned about basic Participles. But there is a "secret" third type of participle structure—a ghost from Ancient Greek that survived in modern, highly formal Katharevousa-style texts. It is called the Genitive Absolute (Η Γενική Απόλυτη Μετοχή) . You will rarely hear this spoken in a coffee shop, but you will absolutely see it in newspapers, official documents, literature, and news broadcasts. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the Genitive Absolute (Noun + Participle), ancient active forms (θέλοντος), and the redoubling in formal passive participles. 1. What is the Genitive Absolute? Sometimes you want to connect two actions that happen at the same time, but they have two different subjects. In normal everyday Greek, you use the word ενώ (while) or επειδή (because): Επειδή έβρεχε, μείναμε μέσα. (Because it was raining, we stayed inside). To sound incredibly formal and elevated, C2 speakers rip out the connector words, erase the verb, and replace the whole clause with Nouns and Participles in the Genitive Case . 2. The Formula & The Grammar Instead of a normal sentence with a Nominative subject and a normal verb, you create an "Absolute" bubble out of Genitive words. Formula: [Noun in Genitive] + [Participle in Genitive] A. The Ancient Active Participles (-ων, -ουσα, -ον) To understand absolute expressions, you must recognize a class of ancient active participles that survive in formal Modern Greek. The stem for the Genitive forms ends in -οντ- . Genitive: του τρέχ οντ ος (of the running one). Plural Genitive: των τρέχ οντ ων (of the running ones). B. Passive Participles & Reduplication (-μένος) In highly formal C2 Greek, many passive participles feature an ancient prefix (an extra syllable) called reduplication . δίνω (I give) ➔ δεδομένος (given). τελώ (I perform) ➔ τετελεσμένος (accomplished). TIP Example in Context: Normal Greek: Επειδή αυτά τα πράγματα είναι δεδομένα... Genitive Absolute: Δεδομένων αυτών των πραγμάτων... (These things being given...) 3. The Most Common Frozen Expressions In Modern Greek, people almost exclusively rely on a handful of highly recognizable, frozen expressions. The Genitive Absolute Literal Translation True Meaning Θεού θέλοντος God willing Hopefully / God willing Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων Things having well All being well / Hopefully Απουσιάζοντος του καθηγητού The teacher being absent With the teacher absent Τρέχοντος του έτους The year running During the current year Μη γένοιτο! Let it not happen God forbid! Τουναντίον... Of the opposite On the contrary... Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων, θα τελειώσουμε το έργο αύριο. (All going well, we will finish the project tomorrow). 🎯 Unit 32 Mastery Checklist I know that the Genitive Absolute is an ancient, highly formal way of connecting two clauses. I understand it is built using a Genitive Subject + a Genitive Participle. I know that active participles use the -οντ- stem in the genitive (e.g., θέλ οντ ος). I recognize reduplication in formal passive participles (like δε δομένος). I have memorized the most common C2 expressions: Θεού θέλοντος and Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων . Practice Vocabulary: C2 Vocabulary: Advanced Particles & Absolute Expressions Up Next: Unit 33: Irregular High-Level Expressions

genitive-case.title: Unit 15: The Genitive Case (Possession & Relationships)
genitive-case.content: Unit 15: The Genitive Case (Possession) Welcome to the B1 Intermediate Stage . You’ve mastered Subjects (Nominative) and Objects (Accusative). Now, we enter the world of Ownership. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the Genitive case structures, the "Universal Plural (των)", and the Shifting Accent rule for possession. The Genitive case is the Greek equivalent of the English apostrophe "s" ('s) or the word "of". It is how you say "my friend's house" or "the color of the car." 1. The "Of" Rule (How to Build It) In English, we usually put the owner first: "George's dog." Greek flips this around completely. You must always use the structure: The Item + of the Owner . Furthermore, both the item and the owner must keep their definite articles! Ο σκύλος του Γιώργου (George's dog / The dog of the George ). Το σπίτι της Μαρίας (Maria's house / The house of the Maria ). Το χρώμα του αυτοκινήτου (The car's color / The color of the car ). 2. The Genitive Articles When a person or thing becomes an "Owner," their definite article must change. There is a beautifully consistent pattern here: Gender The Owner (Singular) The Owners (Plural) Masculine του (too) των (ton) Feminine της (tees) των (ton) Neuter του (too) των (ton) TIP The Universal Plural (των) In the Genitive plural, gender doesn't exist! Every single plural owner uses the article των , and its noun will perfectly rhyme by ending in -ων . Των φίλων (of the friends), των γυναικών (of the women), των παιδιών (of the children). 3. The Noun Endings (Singular) How does the noun itself change to show possession? Masculine Nouns If it ends in -ος , it changes to -ου (ο φίλος ➔ του φίλ ου ). If it ends in -ας or -ης , simply drop the final -ς (ο πατέρας ➔ του πατέρα). (It looks just like the Accusative!) Feminine Nouns Always add a final -ς ! (η Μαρία ➔ της Μαρία ς , η τέχνη ➔ της τέχνη ς ). Neuter Nouns If it ends in -ο , change it to -ου (το βιβλίο ➔ του βιβλί ου ). If it ends in -ι , it morphs into a strong -ιού (το παιδί ➔ του παιδ ιού ). If it ends in -μα , it bulks up by adding an extra syllable, becoming -ματος (το πρόβλημα ➔ του προβλή ματος ). CAUTION The "-ος" Traps Just like in the plural, you must watch out for the disguised nouns! Neuters in -ος: Words like το λάθος (mistake) change to -ους in the Genitive! ( το κράτος ➔ του κράτους ). Feminines in -ος: Words like η οδός (street) take the masculine -ου ending, but fiercely keep their feminine article! ( η μέθοδος ➔ της μεθόδου ). 4. The Shifting Accent (Pronunciation Secret) The Genitive case is famous for moving the stress of a word. If a masculine or neuter noun has its accent on the third syllable from the end (like ο άν -θρω-πος), the weight of the Genitive ending is too heavy. The accent is forced to move one syllable to the right ! ο άν θρωπος (the person) ➔ του αν θρώ που (of the person) το θέ ατρο (the theater) ➔ του θε ά τρου (of the theater) το ό νομα (the name) ➔ του ο νό ματος (of the name) 5. Beyond Ownership The Genitive isn't just for physical items. It is used to express almost any relationship between two nouns: Creators: η μουσική του Μότσαρτ (Mozart's music). Measurements & Age: ένα παιδί τεσσάρων χρονών (a four-year-old child). Contents: ένα ποτήρι νερού (a glass of water). You also learned in Unit 13 that the Genitive is used for Indirect Objects (e.g., Σου δίνω - I give to you). 🎯 Unit 15 Mastery Checklist I know that Greek structures possession as "The Item of the Owner". I can use the correct Genitive articles: του , της , του (singular) and the universal των (plural). I know how to add -ς to feminines, and turn neuter -μα into -ματος . I remember the "-ος" traps: neuters become -ους , feminines become -ου . I know to shift the accent to the right for words like άνθρωπος ➔ ανθρώπου . Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Family Tree & Possessions Next Lesson: Unit 16: The Imperative Mood (Giving Orders)

idioms.title: Unit 28: Idioms & Ancient Remnants
idioms.content: Unit 28: Idioms & Ancient Remnants Welcome to the absolute pinnacle of Greek grammar. To reach true, effortless fluency (B2/C1), you must understand the "ghosts" of the past. Modern Greek is a living, breathing language, but it is built on thousands of years of history. It is filled with ancient expressions that don't follow modern rules, and colorful street idioms that make no literal sense. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on ancient dative remnants (Δόξα τω Θεώ), advanced prepositions, and street idioms like "Βρέχει καρεκλοπόδαρα". 1. Ancient Dative Expressions (The Ghosts) Ancient Greek had a fourth noun case called the "Dative" (used for "to" or "for"). This case completely vanished in modern times—but a few specific phrases survived. You cannot learn the grammar for these; you must simply memorize them as frozen blocks of vocabulary. You will hear them every day: Εν τω μεταξύ (In the meantime / Meanwhile). Δόξα τω Θεώ (Thank God / Glory to God). Εν πάση περιπτώσει (In any case / Anyway). Τοις μετρητοίς (In cash). 2. Advanced Prepositions (Genitive) In Unit 5, you learned that almost all prepositions in modern Greek take the Accusative case ( από τον, με την ). However, formal prepositions—the ones heavily used in the news, business, and politics—are ancient remnants. They stubbornly demand that the noun following them be in the Genitive case! Λόγω + Genitive (Due to): Λόγω βροχής (Due to rain). Μέσω + Genitive (Via / Through): Μέσω Αθηνών (Via Athens). Ενώπιον + Genitive (Before / In front of): Ενώπιον του δικαστηρίου (Before the court). Εξαιτίας + Genitive (Because of): Εξαιτίας του νόμου (Because of the law). 3. Colorful Street Idioms If you translate these directly, you will be deeply confused. These are the idioms that make you sound like a local. Έγινε της κακομοίρας! (A disaster happened! / All hell broke loose!) - Literally: It became the bad-destiny woman's. Χάνω τα λόγια μου. (I am lost for words). Πού το πας; (What are you getting at? / Where are you going with this in the conversation?). Σιγά τα λάχανα! (Big deal! / Who cares!) - Literally: Slowly the cabbages! Βρέχει καρεκλοπόδαρα. (It's raining cats and dogs) - Literally: It's raining chair-legs! Περαστικά! (Get well soon! / May it pass!) 4. Compound "Lego" Words Greek has a superpower: it builds new, massive words by snapping smaller words together. When it does this, it almost always uses the letter "ο" as the glue! If you know the two base words, you can instantly read the massive compound word: τόξο (bow) + βόλο (shoot) ➔ τοξοβόλος (archer). ψάρι (fish) + ταβέρνα (tavern) ➔ ψαροταβέρνα (fish tavern). βιβλίο (book) + πωλείο (shop) ➔ βιβλιοπωλείο (bookstore). 🎯 B2 / C1 Capstone Congratulations! You have navigated the incredibly complex waters of intermediate and advanced Greek grammar. You have officially completed the Masterclass Curriculum. You have mastered: Advanced Particles, Conversational Fillers & Softeners. Relative ( Που, ο οποίος ) and Reflexive ( Ο εαυτός μου ) Pronouns. Comparison of Adjectives ( Πιο vs -τερος ). Active and Passive Participles ( -οντας, -μένος ). The nuances of Impersonal Verbs ( Πρέπει , Μπορεί , Πρόκειται ). Formal Ancient Remnants and Advanced Compound Words. Practice Vocabulary: C1 Vocabulary: Idioms & Society Next Step: Level C: Proficiency (Real-world Dialogue & Literature)

imperative.title: Unit 16: The Imperative Mood (Giving Orders)
imperative.content: Unit 16: The Imperative Mood (Giving Orders) To give an order, a strong piece of advice, or a set of instructions, you use the Imperative Mood . Because you can only give an order directly to the person you are talking to, this mood only has two forms: "You" (informal singular) and "You All" (plural/formal). TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on simple vs. continuous commands, the "Big 6" irregulars, and the polite use of the particle "Για". Just like everything else in Greek, Aspect rules the Imperative. You must choose between ordering a single action (a Photo) or an ongoing habit (a Video). 1. Simple Commands (The Photo: "Do it once!") This is by far the most common way to give a command. You want someone to do something right now, or just once. You build this using the Simple (Perfective) Stem you learned in Unit 12. For Type A Verbs (e.g., γράφω ➔ Simple Stem: γράψ-): Εσύ (Singular): Add -ε to the simple stem. Γράψ ε ! (Write!) Κλείσ ε την πόρτα! (Close the door!) Εσείς (Plural/Formal): Add -τε (if the stem ends in a hard consonant) or -ετε to the simple stem. Γράψ τε ! (Write, you all!) Πλύν ετε τα χέρια σας! (Wash your hands!) TIP Pronunciation Secret (Vowel Dropping) When you give a singular command and follow it with a weak object pronoun (like "it" - το ), native speakers almost always drop the final -ε so they can speak faster! Γράψε το! ➔ Γράψ' το! (Write it!). Φέρε τα! ➔ Φέρ' τα! (Bring them!). 2. Continuous Commands (The Video: "Keep doing it!") Use this when you want to give a general rule, tell someone to keep doing something, or establish a habit. You build this using the Continuous (Present Tense) Stem . For Type A Verbs (e.g., γράφω ➔ Continuous Stem: γράφ-): Εσύ: Add -ε . Γράφ ε καθαρά! (Write cleanly - as a general rule). Τρέχ ε ! (Keep running!). Εσείς: Add -ετε . (Note: This form looks exactly identical to the normal Present tense!) Γράφ ετε . (Keep writing, you all). CAUTION The Shifting Accent If your continuous command has three or more syllables, Greek forces the accent back to the third syllable from the end . Διαβάζω (I read) ➔ Διάβαζε! (Keep reading!). 3. The "Big 6" Irregulars Like most languages, the verbs used most often as commands refuse to follow the rules. You MUST memorize these six singular Simple Commands—you will hear them every single day in Greece: Λέω (Say) ➔ Πες! (plural: Πείτε!) Βλέπω (Look) ➔ Δες! (plural: Δείτε!) Βρίσκω (Find) ➔ Βρες! (plural: Βρείτε!) Πίνω (Drink) ➔ Πιες! (plural: Πιείτε!) Έρχομαι (Come) ➔ Έλα! (plural: Ελάτε!) Πάω / Πηγαίνω (Go) ➔ Πήγαινε! (plural: Πηγαίνετε!) 4. Negative Commands (How to say "Don't!") The Imperative Mood only exists for positive orders ("Do this!"). If you want to say "Don't!", you must use the rule you learned in Unit 10: Subjunctive Verbs take Μη(ν) . To tell someone NOT to do something, simply put Μην in front of the "You" form of the verb. Photo (Just this once): Μην κλείσεις την πόρτα! (Don't close the door!). Video (Habit/General): Μην τρέχεις! (Don't run!). (If you want to shout a single-word command for "Don't!", you just yell: Μη! ) 5. Speak Like a Native: The "Για" Particle If you want to add extra encouragement or friendliness to a positive command, put the tiny particle για right in front of it! It translates roughly to "Go on and..." or "Come on...". Για κάτσε! (Go on and sit down! / Why don't you sit?). Για έλα εδώ. (Come over here for a second). 🎯 Unit 16 Mastery Checklist I know that Simple Commands (Photo) are built from the Simple Stem ( Γράψε! ). I can drop the final vowel when an object pronoun follows ( Γράψ' το! ). I know that Continuous Commands (Video) use the Present Stem ( Γράφε! ). I have memorized the "Big 6" irregular commands ( Πες, Δες, Βες, Πιες, Έλα, Πήγαινε ). I know how to use Μην + the Subjunctive to say "Don't!". I can use Για to soften an order and sound native. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Directions & Commands Next Lesson: Unit 17: Adverbs & Expressions of Manner

impersonal-verbs.title: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions
impersonal-verbs.content: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions Sometimes an action happens, but nobody in particular is doing it. In English, we use a "dummy" subject to fix this: we say " It is raining," or " It is necessary." Greek doesn't use dummy pronouns. Instead, it uses Impersonal Verbs . These verbs have no subject and are completely frozen in the 3rd Person Singular (they end in -ει or -εται ). TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on "Πρέπει" vs. "Μπορεί", the "Πρόκειται" denial, and the native trick of using "Έχει" for "There is". 1. The Power Pair: Πρέπει & Μπορεί The two most important impersonal verbs in the language are πρέπει (it must/ought) and μπορεί (it might/may). They both force the next verb into the Subjunctive ( να ). At the B2 level, you must master their advanced conversational nuances. WARNING Μπορώ vs. Μπορεί Never confuse the two! Μπορώ (Conjugated) = Personal ability. (e.g., Μπορώ να τρέξω - I have the ability to run). Μπορεί (Frozen) = General possibility. (e.g., Μπορεί να βρέξει - It might rain). B2 Nuance 1: Deductive "Must" Greeks use Πρέπει να not just for obligations, but to make strong deductions about reality (the equivalent of "They must have..."). Είδα φως στο σπίτι. Πρέπει να είναι εκεί. (I saw a light. They must be there). Πρέπει να έχουν γυρίσει. (They must have returned). B2 Nuance 2: The Negation Trap Where you place the negative word with μπορεί changes the meaning completely! Μπορεί να μην έρθει. (He might not come - It's a 50/50 possibility). Δεν μπορεί να έρθει. (He cannot come - He lacks the ability). 2. "It's not going to happen": Πρόκειται The verb πρόκειται translates formally to "it is about to" or "the issue is about." But in everyday spoken Greek, it is fiercely used in the negative to state that something is simply not going to happen . Δεν πρόκειται να φύγω! (I am not about to / no way will I leave!). Δεν πρόκειται να τον ξεχάσει ποτέ. (She is never going to forget him). 3. Essential Verbs of Rules & States These verbs are entirely frozen in the -ει or -εται forms. They are heavily used to set rules or express opinions. Απαγορεύεται (It is forbidden): Απαγορεύεται το κάπνισμα. (Smoking is forbidden). Επιτρέπεται (It is allowed): Επιτρέπεται να μπω; (Is it allowed for me to come in?). Φαίνεται (It seems): Φαίνεται ότι θα βρέξει. (It seems that it will rain). Αξίζει (It is worth it): Αξίζει να το δεις. (It is worth seeing). Πειράζει (It matters / bothers): Used constantly in questions! Σε πειράζει αν καπνίσω; (Does it bother you if I smoke?). TIP Native Secret: Impersonal "Έχει" To say "There is" or "There are", textbook Greek teaches you the word Υπάρχει . But on the street, Greeks overwhelmingly just use the frozen word Έχει (literally "It has")! Έχει πολλούς ανθρώπους εδώ. ( There are a lot of people here). Δεν έχει καθόλου ψωμί. ( There is no bread at all). 4. Impersonal "Είναι" Expressions You can take almost any adjective in the Greek language and turn it into a general observation or command by pairing it with Είναι (It is) and a linking word like να (to), ότι (that), or που (that/which). Είναι δύσκολο να μάθεις κινέζικα. (It is difficult to learn Chinese). Είναι κρίμα που έφυγες. (It is a pity that you left). Είναι φανερό ότι λέει ψέματα. (It is obvious that he is lying). Είναι ώρα να πηγαίνουμε. (It is time we get going). 🎯 Unit 27 Mastery Checklist I know that impersonal verbs are permanently frozen in the 3rd person singular ( -ει or -εται ). I can use Πρέπει να to make a strong deduction ( Πρέπει να έφυγαν - They must have left). I totally understand the difference between Μπορεί να μην and Δεν μπορεί να . I can fiercely deny something using Δεν πρόκειται να... (Not about to...). I know the native trick of using Έχει to say "There is" or "There are". I can build general observations using Είναι + [Adjective] + να . Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Expressions, impersonal states & weather Next Lesson: Unit 28: Idioms & Ancient Remnants

interrogative-pronouns.title: Unit 7: Asking Questions
interrogative-pronouns.content: Unit 7: Asking Questions (Τι, Πού, Ποιος) You now have a solid vocabulary and know how to conjugate verbs. The final step in your A1 foundation is learning how to ask for things. In Greek, this involves a special punctuation mark and learning the "W" words. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on fixed vs. chameleon question words, the Greek question mark, and the "Preposition First" rule. 1. The Greek Question Mark (;) The very first thing you must memorize is that the English question mark (?) does not exist in Greek. Instead, the Greek question mark looks exactly like the English semicolon (;). Be careful not to confuse the two when reading! Πώς είσαι; (How are you?) Τι κάνεις; (What are you doing?) 2. The Fixed "W" Words These question words are easy because they are solid blocks. They never change their shape regardless of what you are asking about. Memorize these five: Τι (ti) = What? (Τι είναι αυτό;) Πού (poo) = Where? (Πού είσαι;) Πότε (pó-te) = When? (Πότε φεύγουμε;) Πώς (pos) = How? (Πώς είσαι;) Γιατί (ya-tí) = Why? (Γιατί λες όχι;) TIP The Secret of the Accents You learned in Unit 1 that 1-syllable words don't get accent marks. So why do Πού and Πώς have them? They keep the accent only to differentiate them from their identical twins: που (the relative pronoun "that/which") and πως (the conjunction "that"). This is why 'Τι' doesn't have an accent—it has no twin! 3. The Chameleon "W" Words Just like adjectives (Unit 6), some question words are "chameleons." Since they ask about specific people or things, they must perfectly match the gender, number, and case of the noun you are hunting for. "Who" or "Which" (Ποιος) Pronounced as a single snappy syllable ( pyos ). It acts exactly like a regular adjective. Ποιος είναι ο άντρας; (Who is the man?) Ποια είναι η γυναίκα; (Who is the woman?) Ποιο παιδί; (Which child?) IMPORTANT "Whom" do you see? (The Accusative Alert) If "Who" is the target of the action (the Object), it drops its -ς and adds a -ν , becoming Ποιον . Ποιον βλέπεις; (Whom do you see?) "How Much / How Many" (Πόσος) This word also changes shape depending on what you are counting. Πόσο κάνει; (How much does it cost? - Neuter singular ) Πόση ζάχαρη θέλεις; (How much sugar do you want? - Feminine singular ) Πόσα παιδιά έχεις; (How many children do you have? - Neuter plural ) 4.
