The "Preposition First" Rule In English, it's very common to end a question with a preposition (e.g., "Where did you buy those shoes from ?" or "Who are you talking to ?"). In Greek, you can NEVER separate the preposition from the question word. The preposition must always strictly come first : Από πού είσαι; ( From where are you?) Με ποιον μιλάς; ( With whom are you speaking?) 5. Master the Intonation To turn a normal sentence into a Yes/No question, you don't need to flip the word order or use helper words like "Do" or "Is" (e.g., He is -> Is he? ). You simply keep the exact same word order, slap a Greek question mark ( ; ) at the end, and raise the pitch of your voice on the final word! Statement: Ο Γιώργος είναι εδώ. (George is here.) Question: Ο Γιώργος είναι εδώ; (Is George here?) 🎯 A1 Foundations Capstone Congratulations! You have officially completed the A1 Grammar Foundation . You have successfully mastered: The Greek Writing System and sounds. The Golden Rule of Nouns and the Accusative Case. The "Big Two" Verbs (To Be & To Have) and the Pro-Drop Rule. Conjugating regular Type A Verbs and Negation. The Chameleon Rule of Agreement for Adjectives. Asking anything you want! Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Question Words Next module (A2 Expansion): Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives

irregular-words.title: Unit 33: Irregular & Defective Words
irregular-words.content: Irregular & Defective Words You have mastered the rules of Greek grammar. You know how to decline nouns, conjugate verbs, and build complex sentences. But every language has its rebels—words that refuse to follow the rules, and words that are "defective" (missing pieces!). TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this final podcast on "Gender-Bender" nouns (χρόνος/χρόνια), indeclinable loanwords, and the ancient command form of "I saw" (Ιδού). 1. Nouns with Multiple Personalities (Double Plurals) Some nouns change gender when they become plural, or offer two different plural forms with distinct meanings. The Gender-Benders (Masculine to Neuter) Several common masculine nouns ending in -ος become Neuter ending in -α in the plural. Often, both plural forms exist but mean different things: Ο χρόνος (year/time) ➔ Τα χρόνια (years). (Use "οι χρόνοι" only for grammar tenses!) . Ο λόγος (reason/word) ➔ Τα λόγια (the words). ("Οι λόγοι" = the reasons/speeches) . Ο αδελφός (brother) ➔ Τα αδέλφια (siblings). ("Οι αδελφοί" = specifically brothers) . Ο δεσμός (bond) ➔ Οι δεσμοί (relationships) vs. Τα δεσμά (literal shackles). The "A-La-Carte" Plurals (-ούδες) Loanwords and words ending in stressed vowels often add an extra syllable in the plural: Η αλεπού (fox) ➔ οι αλεπούδες . Η μαϊμού (monkey) ➔ οι μαϊμούδες . Η γλωσσού (gossip) ➔ οι γλωσσούδες . 2. Nouns that Refuse to Decline (Indeclinables) Foreign loanwords often remain frozen. They rely entirely on the Article to show case and number. Το σπορ (The sport) ➔ Βλέπω τα σπορ (I see the sports). Η τζαζ (jazz), η ελίτ (the elite), το ασανσέρ (the lift). Common Gender: ο/η ρεπόρτερ, ο/η μάνατζερ. NOTE Even the letters of the Greek alphabet are neuter indeclinable nouns, like το άλφα and το βήτα ! 3. Defective Verbs (Missing Tenses) A "defective" verb (ελλειπτικό ρήμα) exists exclusively in the imperfective aspect —it lacks a Simple Past (Aorist) or Simple Future. ανήκω (I belong). οφείλω (I owe / I am obliged). τρέμω (I tremble). ξέρω (I know) - Has the imperfect ήξερα , but no Simple Past! Impersonal Ghosts Πρέπει (must) and Πρόκειται (is about to) are frozen in the 3rd person singular. Επρόκειτο: The formal past (Imperfect) of Πρόκειται . 4. The Verb "Είδα" & Ancient Remnants While είδα (I saw) is a standard irregular verb, its ancient command form Ιδού (Behold) survives as a frozen remnant. TIP Famous Proverb: "Ιδού η Ρόδος, ιδού και το πήδημα!" (Behold Rhodes, behold the jump!) — meaning: "Prove it right here, right now!" 🎯 Unit 33 Mastery Checklist I know that some masculine nouns change to Neuter in the plural, often changing their meaning (e.g., λόγια vs. λόγοι ). I understand that loanwords like το σπορ and alphabet letters are indeclinable. I recognize defective verbs that strictly lack perfective tenses ( ανήκω , ξέρω ). I can identify frozen ancient remnants like the command Ιδού .

nouns.title: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns & Articles
nouns.content: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns & Articles Now that you can read Greek, it's time to start building sentences. In English, nouns (words for people, places, and things) are completely static. The word "car" is always "car". In Greek, nouns are alive. They change their shape depending on three things: Gender , Number , and their Case (the role they play in the sentence). TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to the core concepts of gender, articles, and cases explained with native phonetic references. IMPORTANT The Golden Rule of Greek Vocabulary Never learn a Greek noun by itself. Always memorize it with its definite article (the word for "the": ο, η, or το ). The article is the noun's ID card—it tells you the noun's gender and warns you how it will behave in a sentence. 1. Grammatical Gender Every noun in Greek belongs to one of three categories: Masculine , Feminine , or Neuter . Crucially, grammatical gender does not equal biological sex . It's just a category system. For example, the words for "girl" ( το κορίτσι ) and "boy" ( το αγόρι ) are both grammatically neuter! Usually, the last letter of a noun gives away its gender: Gender The Article ("The") Typical Ending Example Translation Masculine ο (o) -ος, -ας, -ης ο φίλος (o fílos) the friend (male) Feminine η (ee) -α, -η η αγάπη (ee agápi) the love Neuter το (to) -ο, -ι, -μα το σπίτι (to spíti) the house WARNING The Tricky "-ος" Ending Most nouns ending in -ος are masculine, but there are exceptions! Some very common words are feminine (like η οδός - the street), and some are neuter (like το λάθος - the mistake). This is exactly why you must follow the Golden Rule and learn the article! 2. The Indefinite Articles ("A" or "An") When you are talking about something non-specific (a car, a house, a friend), you use the indefinite article. Just like the definite article, it has to match the noun's gender. Masculine: ένας ( ένας φίλος - a friend) Feminine: μία / μια ( μια αγάπη - a love) Neuter: ένα ( ένα σπίτι - a house) TIP Pronunciation Secret: The feminine μια is pronounced as a single snappy syllable ( mya ). If you see it written as μία , it is pronounced as two syllables ( mee-a ) and is usually used when you want to strongly emphasize the number "one" rather than just saying "a". 3. Introducing Cases: The Subject vs. The Object In English, "The man" is always "The man," whether he is eating the apple or the apple is hitting him. In Greek, a noun actually changes its shape depending on what it's doing. This is called a "Case". Today, we focus on the two most important cases: The Nominative Case (The Subject) This is the "dictionary form" of the word. You use this case when the noun is doing the action . Ο πατέρας είναι εδώ. (The father is here.) The Accusative Case (The Object) You use this case when the noun is receiving the action (the target of the verb). Here is how nouns transform when they become the Object: Masculine Nouns: Drop the final -ς . The article ο changes to τον . ( Ο πατέρας becomes τον πατέρα ). Feminine Nouns: The noun's ending doesn't change at all! The article η changes to τη(ν) . Neuter Nouns: Total freebie! Neuter nouns and their article ( το ) look exactly the same whether they are the Subject or the Object. Role Masculine Feminine Neuter Subject (Nom) ο πατέρα ς η μητέρα το παιδί Object (Acc) τον πατέρα τη(ν) μητέρα το παιδί NOTE The Disappearing "ν" (Nu) The feminine article την often drops its final 'ν'. You only keep the 'ν' if the next word begins with a vowel or a hard consonant sound (κ, π, τ, γκ, μπ, ντ, ξ, ψ). So, it's τη Δευτέρα (on Monday) but την άνοιξη (in the spring). 4. When to Use "The" Greek uses the definite article ("the") constantly—far more often than English. Here are two massive differences you need to know from day one: Names ALWAYS take articles: You don't say "Maria is here." You say " Η Μαρία είναι εδώ. " (The Maria is here). You don't say "I see Nikos." You say " Βλέπω τον Νίκο. " (I see the Nikos). Abstract Ideas & Broad Categories: Greek uses the article for general truths. "Fear is an instinct" becomes " Ο φόβος είναι ένστικτο " (The fear is an instinct). 🎯 Unit 2 Mastery Checklist I always memorize new vocabulary with its definite article (ο, η, το). I understand that grammatical gender has nothing to do with biological sex. I know the indefinite articles ( ένας, μια, ένα ). I can transform a masculine Subject ( ο φίλος ) into an Object ( τον φίλο ). I remember to put definite articles in front of people's names! Ready to practice? A1 Vocabulary: Family & People Next Lesson: Unit 3: Being & Having

object-pronouns.title: Unit 13: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns (Weak Pronouns)
object-pronouns.content: Unit 13: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns In Unit 3, you learned the subject pronouns (Εγώ, Εσύ, κτλ.) and how Greeks usually drop them. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on Weak Pronouns, the difference between "Me" and "Mou", and the "Stacking Rule" (Person before Thing). Now, we need to talk about the Object Pronouns —the little words like "me," "him," or "it." These are the real stars of everyday Greek conversation. They are short, unstressed, and we call them Weak Pronouns . 1. The Two Types of Targets To use them correctly, you must know if the person is the direct target of the action or the indirect receiver of something. The Direct Object (Accusative Case) This is the person or thing receiving the action directly. Με βλέπεις; (Do you see me ?). Τον ξέρω. (I know him .) The Indirect Object (Genitive Case) This is the person to whom or for whom an action is done. In English, we use extra words like "to" or "for" (e.g., "Give it to me "). In Greek, you don't need "to"—you just use the Genitive version of the pronoun! Μου δίνεις τον καφέ; (Are you giving the coffee to me ?) Σου λέω την αλήθεια. (I am telling the truth to you .) The Pronoun Cheat Sheet English Direct Object (Me/Him/It) Indirect Object (To me/To him) I / me με μου You (sg.) σε σου He / it (masc) τον του She / it (fem) τη(ν) της It (neut) το του We / us μας μας You (pl./formal) σας σας They / them τους / τις / τα τους TIP The Plural Freebies! Notice that for "We" ( μας ) and "You" ( σας ), the Direct and Indirect forms are exactly the same! 2. The Clingy Rule (Positioning) These weak pronouns are incredibly lazy. They cannot stand alone. They must cling immediately to the front of the verb . Το ξέρω. (I know it ). Τον βλέπω. (I see him ). The Particle Sandwich Absolutely nothing can separate these pronouns from their verb—except other pronouns, or the magic particles δεν, μην, θα, να, ας . When you use particles, the pronouns jump inside to be closest to the verb. Δεν σε ξέρω. (I don't know you). Θα σου πω. (I will tell you). Πρέπει να το κάνεις. (You must do it). WARNING The Command Exception! If you are giving a direct command (the Imperative mood), the pronoun is forced to move to the end of the verb and attach to it like a tail! Normal: Μου το λες. (You are telling it to me). Command: Πες μου το! (Tell it to me!). 3. Stacking Pronouns (Person Before Thing) What if you want to say "I give it (the book) to her (Maria)"? You need to use two pronouns at once. Greek is very polite: The Person (Indirect) always comes before the Thing (Direct). Formula: Indirect (Genitive) + Direct (Accusative) + Verb Της το δίνω. (I give it to her ). Μου την δίνεις; (Are you giving her/it to me ?). Σας τα γράφω. (I am writing them to you all ). CAUTION Native Speed: The "S' to" Blend When you stack σου (to you) and το (it), Greeks speak so fast that the "ou" disappears. The phrase σου το is almost always pronounced as a single sharp syllable: σ' το ! Σ' το λέω! (I am telling it to you!) 🎯 Unit 13 Mastery Checklist I can choose between με (Direct/Accusative) and μου (Indirect/Genitive). I know how to put pronouns between particles ( θα, να, δεν ) and the verb. I understand the "Person before Thing" rule for stacking pronouns (e.g., Της το ). I know that command verbs force the pronoun to the end of the word ( Πες μου ). Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Shopping & Clothes Next Lesson: Unit 14: The Past Tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist)

participles.title: Unit 26: Active & Passive Participles (Οι Μετοχές)
participles.content: Unit 26: Active & Passive Participles (Οι Μετοχές) A Participle is a verb dressed up in a costume. It stops acting like a normal verb and starts acting like an adverb (e.g., "He came running ") or an adjective (e.g., "The locked door"). Greek has two powerful participles you must master at the B2 level: the Active Participle (Gerund) and the Passive Perfect Participle. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on Active Gerunds (-οντας/-ώντας) and the Passive Participle suffix (-μένος), plus the spelling "cheat codes". 1. The Active Participle ("While doing") This is an uninflected, frozen word used strictly as an adverb . It translates to the English "-ing" form. It is used to show how someone did something, or to show that two actions are happening at the exact same time. Because it is an adverb, it never changes its shape to match gender or number! Formula: Add the suffix to the Continuous (Present) stem. τρέχω ➔ τρέχοντας (while running / by running). παίζω ➔ παίζοντας (while playing). CAUTION Spelling Rule: -οντας vs. -ώντας How do you know whether to write it with an omicron (ο) or an omega (ω)? It all depends on where you pronounce the stress! 1. If you stress the stem , use -ο- (e.g., τρέχ ο ντας, διαβάζ ο ντας). 2. If you heavily stress the ending (like Type B verbs), use -ώ- (e.g., αγαπ ώ ντας, γελ ώ ντας). Ο αδελφός της ήρθε τρέχοντας . (Her brother came running ). Έφυγε κλαίγοντας . (She left crying ). 2. The Passive Participle (The Completed State) Unlike the active participle, the Passive Perfect Participle acts exactly like a normal adjective (a chameleon!). It describes a completed state resulting from a past action (e.g., Tired, Broken, Written). Because it is an adjective, it perfectly agrees with the noun it modifies in gender, number, and case! Formula: Add the suffix -μένος, -μένη, -μένο to the Passive Simple Stem. κουράζω (tire) ➔ κουρασμένος (tired). χάνω (lose) ➔ χαμένος (lost). Η πόρτα είναι κλεισμένη . (The door is closed ). Τα χαμένα κλειδιά. (The lost keys). TIP Advanced Spelling: The Consonant "Cheat Codes" If you remember the consonant groups from Unit 18, you can perfectly predict how to spell these adjectives! The 'S' Group (ζω, νω, θω): Uses -σμένος (e.g., ξεχνώ ➔ ξεχα σμ ένος). The 'PS' (Lip) Group (πω, βω, φω): The lips close completely, generating a double 'M': -μμένος (e.g., γράφω ➔ γρα μμ ένος, κρύβω ➔ κρυ μμ ένος). The 'X' (Throat) Group (κω, γω, χω): The throat sound softens into a gamma: -γμένος (e.g., ανοίγω ➔ ανοι γμ ένος). 3. Native Exceptions The "Emotions & States" Exception Usually, a verb must be able to take a direct object (like "I wash the car") to have a passive form ("The car is washed"). Emotive or bodily verbs (like "I am hungry") shouldn't have a passive form. However, Greek famously breaks this rule to create some of the most common adjectives in the language! πεινώ (I am hungry) ➔ πεινασμένος (a hungry person). διψώ (I am thirsty) ➔ διψασμένος (a thirsty person). θυμώνω (I get angry) ➔ θυμωμένος (an angry person). αρρωσταίνω (I get sick) ➔ αρρωστημένος (a sick/morbid person). Formal vs. Everyday Verbs (-ευμένος vs -εμένος) For verbs ending in -εύω , there are two valid ways to spell the participle. The formal version keeps the 'υ' ( -ευμένος ), but the informal, everyday street version strips it out completely ( -εμένος ). You should use the everyday version to sound native! παντρεύω (marry) ➔ παντρεμένος (married). μπερδεύω (confuse) ➔ μπερδεμένος (confused). 🎯 Unit 26 Mastery Checklist I can build the active gerund to show simultaneous actions ("while doing"). I know the spelling rule: -οντας (stress on stem) vs. -ώντας (stress on ending). I know that the active gerund is frozen and never changes its ending. I can build the passive adjective ( -μένος ) to describe mapped states. I know that -μένος must match the noun perfectly in gender, number, and case. I can predict spellings like -μμένος and -γμένος based on the verb's consonant. Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Action & States Next Lesson: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions

particles.title: Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Spoken Expressions
particles.content: Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Spoken Expressions Welcome to the C1 Advanced Stage . You now possess all the structural grammar needed to communicate in Greek. Now, it's time to learn the seasoning . To sound truly native, you must master the tiny, untranslatable words that Greeks constantly sprinkle into their speech. These are called particles . They add flavor, doubt, extreme sarcasm, or intense emotion to a sentence. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on softening questions with "Άραγε", using "Τάχα" for sarcasm, and the universal power of "Άντε". 1. Curiosity & Doubt Greeks rarely ask blunt questions. They use these particles to soften their inquiries or express genuine wonder. Άραγε (I wonder...) Placed at the beginning or end of a question, it shows you are pondering out loud. It makes the question sound much less abrupt. Άραγε θα έρθει η Μαρία; (I wonder if Maria will come?) Πού να πήγε, άραγε ; (Where can he have gone, I wonder?). Μήπως (By any chance...) If you want to ask a question politely without sounding accusatory, you start with μήπως . Μήπως σε ενόχλησε; (Did he bother you, by any chance?). Μήπως έχεις στυλό; (Do you happen to have a pen?). 2. Sarcasm & Disbelief Τάχα & Δήθεν (Supposedly / "Yeah, right") Greeks love sarcasm. When someone claims something that you refuse to believe, you throw one of these words into the sentence. Κλαίει δήθεν επειδή χτύπησε. (He is supposedly crying because he got hurt [but I know he's faking]). Ήρθε για να βοηθήσει, τάχα . (He came to help, allegedly ). Ναι, τάχα μου! (Yeah, right!) 3. Wishes & Hopes Μακάρι (If only / Let's hope) This powerful word expresses a deep wish. It almost always triggers the Subjunctive ( να ) right after it. It can also stand completely alone as an answer! Μακάρι να περάσω τις εξετάσεις! (I hope to God I pass the exams!) Person A: Θα βρούμε τραπέζι; ➔ Person B: Μακάρι! (Let's hope so!) Αχ (Oh! / If only!) The ultimate Greek sigh. It is purely an exclamation of emotion, heavily used to express an unfulfilled wish or a complaint. Αχ , να σ' έβλεπα! (Oh, if only I could see you!). Αχ , τι πάθαμε! (Oh, what a disaster we've suffered!). 4. Commands & Resignation Ας (Let / Let it be) You already know that ας means "Let's" ( ας πάμε - let's go). But it is also heavily used to show absolute indifference or resignation. Ας έρθουν αν θέλουν. (Let them come if they want... I don't really care). Ας είναι. (So be it / Let it be). Το "Για" (The Encouragement Particle) You learned this in Unit 16! When placed right in front of an imperative command, the hortatory particle για adds friendly, native encouragement. It translates roughly to "Go on and..." or "Come on...". Για κάτσε ρε φίλε! (Do sit down, my friend!). Για πες μου... (Come on, tell me...). Άντε / Άιντε (Come on!) Perhaps the most versatile and frequently used word in the entire language. Depending on your tone of voice, it can mean: Encouragement: Άντε , μπορείς! (Come on, you can do it!) Impatience: Άντε , σήκω! (Hurry up, get up!) Dismissal: Άντε φύγε από δω. (Get out of here). Resignation: Άντε πάλι... (Oh, here we go again...) 🎯 Unit 22 Mastery Checklist I can soften a blunt question using άραγε or μήπως . I know how to use δήθεν and τάχα to show extreme sarcasm or disbelief. I can enthusiastically agree with a hope by saying Μακάρι! I understand that ας can express permission or complete indifference. I can use Για before a command to sound friendly, and Άντε for literally everything else. Practice Vocabulary: C1 Vocabulary: Spoken Expressions Next Lesson: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (που, ο οποίος)

perfect-tenses.title: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses (I have done)
perfect-tenses.content: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses (I have done) If you made it through the Past tenses, you will find the Perfect tenses incredibly easy. Modern Greek builds these tenses almost exactly like English! You simply combine a Helper Verb ("To Have") with a Frozen Action Word (like "played" or "written"). TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the Present vs. Past Perfect, the active (-ει) vs. passive (-εί) frozen forms, and the Pronoun Magnet rule. 1. The Engine: The Helper Verb (Έχω) In the Perfect tenses, the main verb never changes its shape. All the hard work of showing who is doing the action and when is handled entirely by the helper verb έχω . Depending on the timeline you want, you just choose its Present or Past form: Person Present Helper (I have...) Past Helper (I had...) (Εγώ) έχω είχα (Εσύ) έχεις είχες (Αυτός/ή/ό) έχει είχε (Εμείς) έχουμε είχαμε (Εσείς) έχετε είχατε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) έχουν(ε) είχαν(ε) 2. The Frozen Word (Το Απαρέμφατο) To create the action word (e.g., "played", "read", "written"), you take the Simple Stem (from Unit 12) and attach a specific ending. This special word is called the Non-Finite ( Απαρέμφατο ). It is completely frozen—it NEVER changes, regardless of who is doing the action! The Active Voice Ending: -ει For regular active verbs, add the ending -ει to the active simple stem. The stress must always fall on the second-to-last syllable. διαβάζω (read) ➔ διαβάσ- ➔ διαβάσει γράφω (write) ➔ γράψ- ➔ γράψει ανοίγω (open) ➔ ανοίξ- ➔ ανοίξει The Passive/Deponent Ending: -εί To form the passive perfect, add -εί to the passive simple stem (the one that usually has a θ, στ, or χτ ). Notice that the stress loudly hits the final syllable! χάνομαι (be lost) ➔ χαθ- ➔ χαθεί κοιμάμαι (sleep) ➔ κοιμηθ- ➔ κοιμηθεί WARNING The 1-Syllable Irregulars Do you remember the "Rebel" verbs from Unit 12? Since they shrink down to one syllable in the simple stem, their frozen word is also incredibly short! βλέπω ➔ δει λέω ➔ πει βρίσκω ➔ βρει 3. Assembling the Timeline Now, just stick the Helper Verb and the Frozen Word together! A. Present Perfect (I have done) Used for an action completed in the past that matters right now . Έχω διαβάσει το βιβλίο. (I have read the book). Έχεις κοιμηθεί; ( Have you slept? ). B. Past Perfect / Pluperfect (I had done) Used for an action completed long ago, usually before another past event. Use the past helper ( είχα ). Όταν έφτασα, είχε φύγει . (When I arrived, he had left ). Είχα χαθεί χθες. (I had been lost yesterday). C. Future Perfect (I will have done) Used for an action that will be fully completed before a specific deadline in the future. Just add the future particle ( θα ) to the Present Perfect! Θα έχω τελειώσει μέχρι αύριο. (I will have finished by tomorrow). 4. The Pronoun Magnet Weak object pronouns ("it", "him", "to me") love to cling to the front of the verb engine. In this case, the engine is έχω ! They must completely jump over the frozen word and attach themselves to the front. Το διαβάζω. ➔ Το έχω διαβάσει. (I have read it ). Της μιλάω. ➔ Της είχε μιλήσει. (He had spoken to her ). Δεν το κάνω. ➔ Δεν το έχω κάνει. (I haven't done it ). (Notice how in a negative sentence, the pronoun safely hides between δεν and έχω ). 🎯 Unit 20 Mastery Checklist I can build the active frozen word ( -ει ) and the passive/deponent frozen word ( -εί ). I know that the helper verb ( έχω / είχα ) is the only part of the sentence that conjugates. I can easily translate "I have played", "I had played", and "I will have played". I remember to put weak object pronouns before the helper verb ( Το έχω κάνει ). Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Travel & Life Experiences Next Lesson: Unit 21: Conditionals (The 'If' Clauses)

plurals.title: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives
plurals.content: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives Welcome to the A2 Expansion Stage ! You’ve mastered the singular world. Now, it’s time to multiply everything. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on plural articles, the masculine "-oi" and "-ous" split, and the "Extra Syllable" rule for words like καφές. In English, making a plural is boring: you just add an "s" to the end of a noun. In Greek, the entire sentence must adapt. When a noun becomes plural, its Article , its Ending , and its Adjective must all change shape to match. 1. Masculine Plurals (Οι / Τους) Masculine nouns are the most hyperactive when they become plural. They have different endings depending on whether they are the Subject (doing the action) or the Object (receiving the action). Role Article Typical Ending Example Translation Subject (Nom) Οι (ee) -οι, -ες Οι φίλοι The friends Object (Acc) Τους (toos) -ους, -ες Βλέπω τους φίλους I see the friends CAUTION The -οι vs. -ους Trap For masculine nouns ending in -ος (like φίλος ), the ending perfectly rhymes with its article! When they do the action: Οι καλ οί φίλ οι (The good friends). When they receive it: Βλέπω τους καλ ούς φίλ ους (I see the good friends). TIP The "Extra Syllable" Masculines Many everyday nouns ending in a stressed vowel plus -ς ( -άς, -ής, -ές, -ούς ) bulk up in the plural by adding the extra syllable -δες . ο παπάς -> οι παπάδες (the priests) ο καφές -> οι καφέδες (the coffees) 2. Feminine Plurals (Οι / Τις) Feminine nouns are much friendlier. The noun ending never changes between the subject and the object—only the article flips! Role Article Typical Ending Example Translation Subject (Nom) Οι (ee) -ες Οι μητέρες The mothers Object (Acc) Τις (tees) -ες Βλέπω τις μητέρες I see the mothers WARNING The Masked Feminines (-ος) Remember η οδός (the street)? These are feminine nouns masquerading in masculine clothing. In the plural, they take masculine endings ( -οι, -ους ), but they fiercely guard their feminine articles! Subject: Οι οδ οί (The streets). Object: Τις οδ ούς (The streets). 3. Neuter Plurals (Τα) Neuter nouns are the most stable of all. Both their article ( τα ) and their noun endings remain exactly the same whether they are hitting the ball or the ball is hitting them. Singular Plural (Subject & Object) Rule το βιβλίο τα βιβλία -ο ➔ -α το παιδί τα παιδιά -ι ➔ -ια το μάθημα τα μαθήματα -μα ➔ -ματα IMPORTANT The Ultimate Trap: Neuter Nouns in -ος There is a huge group of neuter nouns ending in -ος (like το λάθος - the mistake, το κράτος - the state). In the plural, they drop the -ος and add -η . Do NOT confuse these with masculine nouns! το λάθος ➔ τα λάθη (the mistakes). το δάσος ➔ τα δάση (the forests). 4. The Final Equation: Full Agreement When the noun goes plural, everyone else must follow. The Subject, Adjective, and Verb must agree. Singular: Ο καλ ός φίλ ος διαβάζ ει . (The good friend reads). Plural (Masc): Οι καλ οί φίλ οι διαβάζ ουν . (The good friends read). Plural (Neut): Τα μικρ ά παιδι ά παίζ ουν . (The small children play). 🎯 Unit 8 Mastery Checklist I can pluralize the articles depending on their case: οι/τους (Masc), οι/τις (Fem), and τα (Neut). I know that masculine -ος nouns uniquely split into -οι (subject) and -ους (object). I understand the "Extra Syllable" rule ( -δες ) for words like καφές -> καφέδες . I can correctly pluralize neuter nouns ending in -μα ( μαθήματα ) and -ος ( λάθη ). I can make an entire sentence agree in the plural. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & Hobbies Next Lesson: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2

reading-unit-1.title: Reading 1: The Writing System
reading-unit-1.content: Chapter 1: The Concrete Sea (Η Τσιμεντένια Θάλασσα) NOTE Context / Πλαίσιο You are about to read the first chapter of The Athens Enigma . Nestoras has just arrived at the central station in Athens. He is not here for tourism; he is looking for an old family secret hidden in the chaotic, modern concrete jungle of the city. Πρόκειται να διαβάσετε το πρώτο κεφάλαιο από το "Αίνιγμα της Αθήνας". Ο Νέστορας μόλις έφτασε στον κεντρικό σταθμό της Αθήνας. Δεν είναι εδώ για τουρισμό, αλλά για να ανακαλύψει ένα παλιό οικογενειακό μυστικό κρυμμένο στη ζούγκλα του τσιμέντου. Focus: Bold : Special vowel combos ( ει, αι, οι, ου ) Italics : Consonant digraphs ( μπ, ντ, γκ, τσ, τζ ) Το ταξίδι. The journey. Το τρένο φτάν ει στον σταθμό Λαρίσης. The train arrives at Larissa Station. Ο Νέστορας κοιτάζ ει έξω από το παράθυρο. Nestoras looks out the window. Δεν υπάρχ ου ν λευκά σπίτ ια εδώ, μόνο πολυκατοικ ίε ς. There are no white houses here, only apartment blocks. Η Αθήνα εί ναι μια τσ ιμεντένια θάλασσα. Athens is a concrete sea. Βγαίν ει από το τρένο κ αι νιώθ ει τη ζέστη. He gets off the train and feels the heat. Στην τσ άντα του, έχ ει ένα παλιό τζ άκετ κ αι ένα μπ λοκ σημειώσεων. In his bag, he has an old jacket and a notepad. Ξαφνικά, ένας άντρας με γκ ρίζα μαλλιά τού μιλά ει . Suddenly, a man with grey hair speaks to him. Γ εια σ ου ! Hello! Ψάχν ει ς το ξενοδοχ εί ο; Are you looking for the hotel? ρωτά ει ο άντρας. asks the man. Ο Νέστορας λέ ει : Ναι, εντ άξ ει . Nestoras says: Yes, okay. Πού εί ναι; Where is it? Ο άντρας δείχν ει τον ου ρανό. The man points to the sky. Εκ εί π ου γεννιέται η μπ όρα, λέ ει κρυπτογραφικά . There where the storm is born, he says cryptically. Ο Νέστορας μέν ει σιωπηλός. Nestoras remains silent. Το αί νιγμα ξεκινά ει . The enigma begins. 🔍 Reading Analysis Did you spot the patterns from Unit 1? Vowel Combinations (The "EE" Sound): The words φτάν ει , κοιτάζ ει , εί ναι, and ξενοδοχ εί ο all use ει to make the "EE" sound. Vowel Combinations (The "EH" Sound): The words κ αι and αί νιγμα use αι to make the "EH" sound (like "egg"). Vowel Combinations (The "OO" Sound): The words υπάρχ ου ν, ου ρανό, and σ ου use ου para formar el sonido "U" (como en "luna"). Consonant Digraphs: Did you notice the τσ in τσ ιμεντένια, the τζ in τζ άκετ, the μπ in μπ λοκ, the γκ in γκ ρίζα, and the ντ in εντ άξει? Rule Reminder: Whenever you see these combinations, they merge to form a single, completely new sound. Do not pronounce their letters separately!

reading-unit-10.title: Reading 10: The Search in the Attic
reading-unit-10.content: Chapter 10: The Search in the Attic (Η Αναζήτηση στη Σοφίτα) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio Nestoras returns to his family's old house in the Plaka district. He climbs the narrow stairs to the attic. Everything is covered in dust. He looks around carefully, moving objects to find the next clue. Ο Νέστορας επιστρέφει στο παλιό σπίτι της οικογένειάς του στην Πλάκα. Ανεβαίνει τις στενές σκάλες για τη σοφίτα. Τα πάντα είναι καλυμμένα με σκόνη. Κοιτάζει γύρω του προσεκτικά, μετακινώντας αντικείμενα για να βρει το επόμενο στοιχείο. Focus: Adverbs of Place : Words that describe where something is located. Spatial Relationships : εδώ (here), εκεί (there), πάνω (up), κάτω (down), μέσα (inside), κ.λπ. Εδώ εί ναι η σοφίτα . Here is the attic. Κοιτάζω πάνω στα ράφια . I look up on the shelves. Δεν υπάρ χ ει τ ί ποτα εκεί . There is nothing there. Ψάχνω κάτω από το παλιό τραπέζι . I search under the old table. Μπροστά μ ου εί ναι ένα μεγάλο μπαούλο . In front of me is a large trunk. Κοίτα πίσω από το κουτί ! λ έ ει η γυναίκα . Look behind the box! says the woman. Μέσα στο βιβλίο , υπάρ χ ει ένας χάρτης . Inside the book, there is a map. Βγαίνω έξω στο μπαλκόνι . I go out onto the balcony. Η θάλασσα εί ναι μακριά , αλλά η Ακρόπολη εί ναι κοντά . The sea is far, but the Acropolis is near. 🔍 Reading Analysis Adverbs of Place are essential for describing locations and directions. In Greek, many of these adverbs are used alone or with prepositions. Greek Adverb English Meaning Opposite Εδώ Here Εκεί (There) Πάνω Up / On Κάτω (Down / Under) Μέσα Inside Έξω (Outside) Μπροστά In front Πίσω (Behind) Κοντά Near Μακριά (Far) Usage Note: When using adverbs like πάνω , κάτω , μπροστά , πίσω , κοντά , μακριά with a noun, we usually add the preposition από or σε . Πάνω στο τραπέζι (On the table) Κοντά στο σπίτι (Near the house) Μακριά από την πόλη (Far from the city)

reading-unit-11.title: Reading 11: The Port of Piraeus
reading-unit-11.content: Chapter 11: The Port of Piraeus (Το Λιμάνι του Πειραιά) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio Nestoras studies the old map. He discovers a date and a specific time. Today is Monday. The secret meeting is on Tuesday at eight o'clock in Piraeus. He doesn't have much time. He runs to the port to find the boat. Ο Νέστορας μελετάει τον παλιό χάρτη. Ανακαλύπτει μια ημερομηνία και μια συγκεκριμένη ώρα. Σήμερα είναι Δευτέρα. Η μυστική συνάντηση είναι την Τρίτη στις οκτώ η ώρα στον Πειραιά. Δεν έχει πολύ χρόνο. Τρέχει στο λιμάνι για να βρει το πλοίο. Focus: Days of the Week : Δευτέρα, Τρίτη, Τετάρτη... Telling Time : Τι ώρα είναι; Είναι οκτώ. Time Expressions : Σήμερα (Today), Αύριο (Tomorrow), Τώρα (Now). Σήμερα εί ναι Δε υτέρ α . Today is Monday. Τι ώρ α εί ναι; ρ ω τ ά ει ο Ν έ στορ α ς. What time is it? Nestoras asks. Εί ναι ε π τ ά η ώρ α το βρ ά δ υ . It is seven o'clock in the evening. Το πλ οί ο φ εύ γ ει α ύρ ιο , Τρ ί τ η . The boat leaves tomorrow, Tuesday. Η σ υ ν ά ντ η σ η εί ναι στ ις οκτ ώ το πρ ωί . The meeting is at eight in the morning. Τετ ά ρτ η , Π έ μ πτ η ... δεν έ χ ουμε χρ ό νο! Wednesday, Thursday... we don't have time! Φτ ά ν ει στο λ ιμ ά ν ι το υ Π ει ρ α ι ά . He arrives at the port of Piraeus. Πο ύ εί ναι το πλ οί ο; Where is the boat? Τ ώρ α εί ναι η στ ιγ μ ή για τη ν Α ί γ ιν α . Now is the moment for Aegina. 🔍 Reading Analysis Days of the Week (Οι Ημέρες της Εβδομάδας): In Greek, the days start with Monday. Notice that Monday to Thursday are derived from numbers (2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th)! Δευτέρα (Monday) Τρίτη (Tuesday) Τετάρτη (Wednesday) Πέμπτη (Thursday) Παρασκευή (Friday) Σάββατο (Saturday) Κυριακή (Sunday) Telling Time (Η Ώρα): Τι ώρα είναι; (What time is it?) Είναι μία (It's one) — Feminine, because "ώρα" is feminine! Είναι δύο, τρεις, τέσσερις... (It's two, three, four...) Στις οκτώ (At eight) — We use the preposition "στις" (at) for most hours. Time Phrases: Το πρωί (In the morning) Το μεσημέρι (At noon) Το απόγευμα (In the afternoon) Το βράδυ (In the evening / at night)

reading-unit-12.title: Reading 12: The Storm at Sea
reading-unit-12.content: Chapter 12: The Storm at Sea (Η Καταιγίδα στη Θάλασσα) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio The ferry boat leaves for Aegina. But the weather in the Saronic Gulf is not good. It's autumn and the sky is grey. It's very windy and it's starting to rain. Nestoras is inside, but he wants to see the horizon. Το πλοίο φεύγει για την Αίγινα. Αλλά ο καιρός στον Σαρωνικό δεν είναι καλός. Είναι φθινόπωρο και ο ουρανός είναι γκρίζος. Έχει πολύ αέρα και αρχίζει να βρέχει. Ο Νέστορας είναι μέσα, αλλά θέλει να δει τον ορίζοντα. Focus: Weather Expressions : How to describe the weather. The Seasons : Άνοιξη, Καλοκαίρι, Φθινόπωρο, Χειμώνας. Τι κ αι ρ ό ς εί ναι; ρ ω τ ά ει η γ υ ν αι κ α . What is the weather like? the woman asks. Εί ναι Φθινόπωρο κ αι κ ά ν ει κρ ύ ο. It is Autumn and it is cold. Σήμερα έχει πολ ύ α έ ρ α στη θ ά λ ασσ α . Today it is very windy at sea. Κοίτ α ! βρ έ χ ει τώρ α έξω . Look! it is raining now outside. Δεν έχει ήλ ιο π ια . It doesn't have sun anymore. Το καλοκ αί ρ ι εί ναι μακρι ά . The summer is far. Ότα ν χιον ί ζ ει , το νησ ί εί ναι λ ευ κ ό . When it snows, the island is white. Κ ά ν ει ζ έ στ η στη ν Άνο ιξ η ; Is it hot in the Spring? Ο κ αι ρ ό ς δεν εί ναι φ ί λ ος μ ας σήμερα . The weather is not our friend today. 🔍 Reading Analysis Describing the Weather (Ο Καιρός): In Greek, we use different verbs to describe the weather: Κάνει... (It makes...) Κάνει ζέστη (It's hot) Κάνει κρύο (It's cold) Έχει... (It has...) Έχει ήλιο (It's sunny) Έχει αέρα (It's windy) Έχει συννεφιά (It's cloudy) Verbs: Βρέχει (It's raining) Χιονίζει (It's snowing) The Seasons (Οι Εποχές): 1. Η Άνοιξη (Spring) 2. Το Καλοκαίρι (Summer) 3. Το Φθινόπωρο (Autumn) 4. Ο Χειμώνας (Winter) Grammar Hint: Notice that each season has its own gender! Neuter for Summer/Autumn, Feminine for Spring, and Masculine for Winter.

reading-unit-13.title: Reading 13: The Way to the Temple
reading-unit-13.content: Chapter 13: The Way to the Temple (Ο Δρόμος για τον Ναό) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio The boat arrives at the port of Aegina. The storm is over, but the ground is wet. Nestoras needs to find the Temple of Aphaia. He asks an old man for the way. The temple is far, and he must choose how to get there. Το πλοίο φτάνει στο λιμάνι της Αίγινας. Η καταιγίδα τελείωσε, αλλά το έδαφος είναι υγρό. Ο Νέστορας πρέπει να βρει τον Ναό της Αφαίας. Ρωτάει έναν ηλικιωμένο για τον δρόμο. Ο ναός είναι μακριά, και πρέπει να διαλέξει πώς θα πάει εκεί. Focus: Transport : How to say "by bus", "by car", "on foot". Directions : Giving and receiving directions (left, right, straight). Questions : Asking "How do I go to...?" Πώς π άω στο ν Να ό της **Αφα ί ας ; How do I go to the Temple of Aphaia? Πρ έπ ει να π άτε ί σ ια , λέ ει ο ά ντρ ας . You must go straight, says the man. Μετ ά , στρ ί ψτε αρ ιστ ερ ά . Afterwards, turn left. Μ ή π ως εί ν αι δεξ ι ά ο δρ ό μ ος ; Is the road to the right, perhaps? Ό χ ι , εί ν αι πολ ύ μακρ ι ά α π ό εδ ώ . No, it is very far from here. Π αγ αί νετε με το λε ωφ ορ εί ο; Are you going by bus? Ό χ ι , θ έ λ ω να π άω με τα π ό δ ια . No, I want to go on foot. Πο ύ εί ν αι η στ ά σ η για το ταξ ί**; Where is the stop for the taxi? Εί ν αι δί πλ α στο λ ιμ ά ν ι . It is next to the port. 🔍 Reading Analysis Means of Transport (Μεταφορικά Μέσα): In Greek, we use the preposition με (with/by) plus the article and the noun to show how we travel. Με το λεωφορείο (By bus) Με το ταξί (By taxi) Με το πλοίο (By boat) Με το αυτοκίνητο (By car) Με τα πόδια (On foot — literally: "with the feet" ) Giving Directions (Οδηγίες): Ίσια (Straight) Αριστερά (Left) Δεξιά (Right) Στρίψτε (Turn — formal/plural ) Στρίψε (Turn — informal ) Useful Phrases: Πώς πάω στο...; (How do I go to...?) Πού είναι η στάση; (Where is the stop?) Είναι κοντά / Είναι μακριά (It's near / It's far)

reading-unit-2.title: Reading 2: The Mysterious Note
reading-unit-2.content: Chapter 2: The Silver Key (Το Ασημένιο Κλειδί) NOTE Context / Πλαίσιο Nestoras follows the man's directions. He finds a small, hidden hotel near the station. It is old, but it feels safe. He receives a silver key from the receptionist. Inside his room, he finds something unexpected. Ο Νέστορας ακολουθεί τις οδηγίες του άντρα. Βρίσκει ένα μικρό, κρυφό ξενοδοχείο κοντά στον σταθμό. Είναι παλιό, αλλά νιώθει ασφαλής. Παίρνει ένα ασημένιο κλειδί από τον υπάλληλο. Μέσα στο δωμάτιό του, βρίσκει κάτι απροσδόκητο. Focus: ο / η / το : The Definite Articles (Masculine / Feminine / Neuter). Endings : Notice how nouns change based on their gender. Ο δρόμος εί ναι σκοτεινός. The road is dark. Το ξενοδοχ εί ο εί ναι παλιό. The hotel is old. Ο υπάλληλος δίν ει το κλειδί. The employee gives the key. Η πόρτα εί ναι ξ ύλινη. The door is wooden. Ο Νέστορας μπ αίν ει στο δωμάτ ιο . Nestoras enters the room. Το κρε β άτι εί ναι μικρό. The bed is small. Η λά μπ α εί ναι αναμμένη. The lamp is on. Πάνω στο τραπέζ ι , υπάρχει ο χάρτης. On the table, there is the map. Δί πλ α στον χάρτη, η σημείωση: Next to the map, the note: " Ο χρόνος τρέχ ει , Νέστορα. Το μυστικό περιμέν ει ." "Time is running, Nestoras. The secret is waiting." 🔍 Reading Analysis In this chapter, we focus on Nouns & Articles . Greek has three genders, and each has its own article: Masculine (ο): Ο δρόμος (the road), ο υπάλληλος (the employee), ο χάρτης (the map), ο χρόνος (the time). Feminine (η): Η πόρτα (the door), η λάμπα (the lamp), η σημείωση (the note). Neuter (το): Το ξενοδοχείο (the hotel), το κλειδί (the key), το δωμάτιο (the room), το μυστικό (the secret). Grammar Hint: Notice how masculine nouns often end in -ος or -ης , feminine in -α or -η , and neuter in -ο or -ι . Identifying the gender is the first step to mastering the Greek case system!

reading-unit-3.title: Reading 3: The Room 303
reading-unit-3.content: Chapter 3: Room 303 (Δωμάτιο 303) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio Nestoras is now in his room. He is tired, but he has much work to do. He looks at the objects in the room. He is alone, but he feels that someone was here before him. Ο Νέστορας είναι τώρα στο δωμάτιό του. Είναι κουρασμένος, αλλά έχει πολλή δουλειά. Κοιτάζει τα αντικείμενα στο δωμάτιο. Είναι μόνος, αλλά νιώθει ότι κάποιος ήταν εδώ πριν από αυτόν. Focus: είμαι : To Be (I am, you are, he/she/it is). έχω : To Have (I have, you have, he/she/it has). Είμαι στο δωμάτ ιο 303. I am in room 303. Είσαι εδώ, Νέστορα; ρωτά ει τον εαυτό του. Are you here, Nestoras? he asks himself. Είναι ένα δωμάτ ιο σκοτεινό κ αι κρύο. It is a room dark and cold. Έχω την τσ άντα μ ου πάνω στο κρε β άτι. I have my bag on the bed. Έχ εις το μυστικό; ψιθ υ ρίζ ει . Do you have the secret? he whispers. Έχ ει μια παλιά φωτογραφ ία στον τ οί χο. It has an old photo on the wall. Είμαστε μόνοι στο ξενοδοχ εί ο. We are alone in the hotel. Έχουν το κλειδί, αλλά έχω το αίνιγμα. They have the key, but I have the enigma. Είσαι έτ οι μος; Are you ready? 🔍 Reading Analysis In this lesson, we use the two most important verbs in Greek: είμαι (to be) and έχω (to have). Person είμαι (To Be) έχω (To Have) I είμαι έχω You (sing.) είσαι έχεις He/She/It είναι έχει We είμαστε έχουμε You (plur./formal) είστε έχετε They είναι έχουν Note: Notice how είναι is used for both "he/she/it is" and "they are". Context tells you which one it is!

reading-unit-4.title: Reading 4: The Cafe in Monastiraki
reading-unit-4.content: Chapter 4: The Cafe in Monastiraki (Το Καφενείο στο Μοναστηράκι) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio Nestoras walks through Monastiraki. It is a busy place. He finds a traditional cafe. He sits and observes the people. He looks for a sign, but he doesn't see anything yet. He knows that the search is difficult. Ο Νέστορας περπατάει στο Μοναστηράκι. Είναι ένα πολυσύχναστο μέρος. Βρίσκει ένα παραδοσιακό καφενείο. Κάθεται και παρατηρεί τον κόσμο. Ψάχνει για ένα σημάδι, αλλά δεν βλέπει τίποτα ακόμα. Ξέρει ότι η αναζήτηση είναι δύσκολη. Focus: Verbs (-ω) : Regular verbs in the present tense. Negation (δεν) : How to say "I don't" or "he doesn't". Περπατώ στον δρόμο. I walk on the road. Βλέπω τον κόσμο, αλλά δεν βλέπω το σημάδι. I see the people, but I don't see the sign. Θέλω ένα καφέ, παρακαλώ. I want a coffee, please. Πίνω το ν καφέ μ ου σιγά-σιγά. I drink my coffee slowly. Ακούω τη μ ου σική από το δρόμο. I hear the music from the street. Κοιτάζω το ρολό ι μ ου . I look at my watch. Ξέρεις π ού εί ναι; ρωτά ει το ν σερ β ιτόρο. Do you know where it is? he asks the waiter. Ο σερ β ιτόρος δεν ξέρει . The waiter doesn't know. Δεν έχουμε χρόνο, Νέστορα, σκέφτεται. We don't have time, Nestoras, he thinks. Περιμένω ακόμα. I am still waiting. 🔍 Reading Analysis In this chapter, we introduce Regular Verbs (Type A) . These verbs end in -ω and are the foundation of Greek action. Person Ending Example: βλέπω (to see) I -ω βλέπω You (sing.) -εις βλέπεις He/She/It -ει βλέπει We -ουμε βλέπουμε You (plur.) -ετε βλέπετε They -ουν(ε) βλέπουν Negation: To negate a verb, simply add δεν before it. Ξέρω (I know) → Δεν ξέρω (I don't know). Έχω (I have) → Δεν έχω (I don't have). Note: If the verb starts with a vowel or certain consonants (κ, π, τ, ξ, ψ, γκ, μπ, ντ), the "ν" in δεν is kept. Otherwise, it is sometimes dropped in spoken Greek, but keeping it is always grammatically correct!

reading-unit-5.title: Reading 5: The Museum Shadow
reading-unit-5.content: Chapter 5: The Museum Shadow (Η Σκιά στο Μουσείο) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio Nestoras follows a clue to the National Archaeological Museum. He enters the large hall. He sees the statues, but he also sees a mysterious shadow behind a column. He follows the person into the secret basement. Ο Νέστορας ακολουθεί ένα στοιχείο στο Εθνικό Αρχαιολογικό Μουσείο. Μπαίνει στη μεγάλη αίθουσα. Βλέπει τα αγάλματα, αλλά βλέπει επίσης μια μυστηριώδη σκιά πίσω από μια κολόνα. Ακολουθεί το άτομο στο μυστικό υπόγειο. Focus: Accusative (τον, την, το) : Using the direct object case. Prepositions (στο, στη, στον) : "To the" or "in the". Πηγαίνω στο μ ου σ εί ο. I go to the museum. Μπ αίνω στη μεγάλη αί θ ου σα. I enter the large hall. Βλέπω το άγαλμα, αλλά δεν βλέπω την έξοδο. I see the statue, but I don't see the exit. Ξαφνικά, βλέπω το ν άντρα με τα γκ ρίζα μαλλ ιά . Suddenly, I see the man with the grey hair. Ακολ ου θώ τη σκι ά πίσω α πό την κολόνα. I follow the shadow behind the column. Ο άντρας πηγαίν ει στο υ πό γ ει ο. The man goes to the basement. Κοιτάζω το πάτωμα κ αι το είδωλο στο ν καθρέφτη. I look at the floor and the image in the mirror. Ψάχνω τη λ ύ ση για το α ί νιγμα. I look for the solution for the enigma. Μπ αίν ουμε στο σκότος. We enter the darkness. 🔍 Reading Analysis In this chapter, we use the Accusative Case . This is the case used for the direct object of a sentence (who/what is receiving the action). Gender Nominative (Subject) Accusative (Object) Combined with "σε" (to/in) Masculine ο άντρας το ν άντρα στο ν άντρα Feminine η σκιά τη σκιά στη σκιά Neuter το άγαλμα το άγαλμα στο άγαλμα Grammar Hint: When "σε" (to/at/in) meets the article, they merge into one word! σε + τον = στον σε + την = στη (or στην) σε + το = στο Notice how the neuter το stays the same, while masculine and feminine articles change slightly or lose/gain the final -ν .

reading-unit-6.title: Reading 6: The Golden Door
reading-unit-6.content: Chapter 6: The Golden Door (Η Χρυσή Πόρτα) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio Nestoras is in the secret basement of the museum. He sees a heavy, golden door. On a small table, there are three mysterious boxes: a red one, a blue one, and a black one. He needs to choose the right one to continue his journey. Ο Νέστορας είναι στο μυστικό υπόγειο του μουσείου. Βλέπει μια βαριά, χρυσή πόρτα. Πάνω σε ένα μικρό τραπέζι, υπάρχουν τρία μυστηριώδη κουτιά: ένα κόκκινο, ένα μπλε και ένα μαύρο. Πρέπει να διαλέξει το σωστό για να συνεχίσει το ταξίδι του. Focus: Adjectives : Words that describe nouns (matching gender & case). Colors : χρυσός (golden), κόκκινος (red), μπλε (blue), μαύρος (black). Numbers : ένα (one), δύο (two), τρία (three). Βλέπω τη χρυσή π ό ρτα. I see the golden door. Η π ό ρτα εί ναι μεγάλη κ αι βαριά . The door is large and heavy. Εκεί υπάρ χ ουν τρία μικρά κ ου τι ά . There are three small boxes. Το πρώτο κ ου τ ί εί ναι κόκκινο . The first box is red. Το δεύτερο κ ου τ ί εί ναι μπλε . The second box is blue. Το τρίτο κ ου τ ί εί ναι μαύρο . The third box is black. Π οι ο εί ναι το σωστό κ ου τ ί ; Which is the right box? Το χρυσό κλειδ ί εί ναι μεσα στο μαύρο κ ου τ ί . The golden key is inside the black box. Τ ώρα , έχω τη λ ύ ση. Now, I have the solution. 🔍 Reading Analysis In Greek, Adjectives must agree with the noun they describe in Gender , Number , and Case . Gender Adjective (Nominative) Example Masculine σωστός ο σωστός άντρας Feminine σωστή η σωστή πόρτα Neuter σωστό το σωστό κουτί Colors: Most colors act like regular adjectives (ending in -ος/-η/-ο). Κόκκινος, κόκκινη, κόκκινο (Red) Μαύρος, μαύρη, μαύρο (Black) Χρυσός, χρυσή, χρυσό (Golden) Μπλε (Blue) — Note: Some foreign colors like "μπλε" or "ροζ" are indeclinable (they don't change)! Numbers: The numbers 1, 3, and 4 also change gender! Ένας (M), μία (F), ένα (N) Τρεις (M/F), τρία (N) Τέσσερις (M/F), τέσσερα (N)

reading-unit-7.title: Reading 7: The Syntagma Interview
reading-unit-7.content: Chapter 7: The Syntagma Interview (Η Συνέντευξη στο Σύνταγμα) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio Nestoras is now at Syntagma Square, the heart of Athens. He has the key, but he needs to find the right person. He sees a woman with a red scarf. He decides to ask her some questions. Ο Νέστορας είναι τώρα στην Πλατεία Συντάγματος, στην καρδιά της Αθήνας. Έχει το κλειδί, αλλά πρέπει να βρει το σωστό άτομο. Βλέπει μια γυναίκα με κόκκινο κασκόλ. Αποφασίζει να της κάνει μερικές ερωτήσεις. Focus: Question Words : Ποιος (Who), Τι (What), Πού (Where), Πότε (When), Γιατί (Why), Πώς (How). The Question Mark : In Greek, we use the semicolon (;) as a question mark. Ποιος είναι ο άντρας με τα γκρίζα μαλλιά; ρωτάει ο Νέστορας. Who is the man with the grey hair? Nestoras asks. Τι είναι αυτό το κλειδί; What is this key? Πού είναι το μυστικό πέρασμα; Where is the secret passage? Πότε είναι η συνάντηση; When is the meeting? Γιατί είστε εδώ στην πλατεία; Why are you here in the square? Πώς ξέρετε το όνομά μου; How do you know my name? Η γυναίκα γελάει. Τι θέλεις να μάθεις; The woman laughs. What do you want to learn? Ποια είναι η αλήθεια; ρωτάει ξανά. What is the truth? he asks again. Πώς μπορούμε να ανοίξουμε την πόρτα; How can we open the door? 🔍 Reading Analysis In Greek, asking questions is very similar to English, but we use different words and a unique punctuation mark: the semicolon (;) . Question Word Meaning Example Ποιος/Ποια/Ποιο Who / Which Ποιος είναι; (Who is it?) Τι What Τι κάνεις; (What are you doing?) Πού Where Πού είσαι; (Where are you?) Πότε When Πότε φεύγουμε; (When are we leaving?) Γιατί Why Γιατί όχι; (Why not?) Πώς How Πώς είσαι; (How are you?) Πόσο How much Πόσο κάνει; (How much does it cost?) Note: The question mark in Greek is NOT the symbol "?". It is the ; symbol! Τι κάνεις? (Wrong) Τι κάνεις; (Correct) Intonation: Just like in English, the voice usually goes up at the end of a question!

reading-unit-8.title: Reading 8: The Hidden Documents
reading-unit-8.content: Chapter 8: The Hidden Documents (Τα Κρυφά Έγγραφα) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio With the black key, Nestoras opens a hidden drawer. Inside, there are many objects from the past. He finds old documents, many photographs, and secret notes. He realizes that many people are looking for the same thing. Με το μαύρο κλειδί, ο Νέστορας ανοίγει ένα κρυφό συρτάρι. Μέσα, υπάρχουν πολλά αντικείμενα από το παρελθόν. Βρίσκει παλιά έγγραφα, πολλές φωτογραφίες και κρυφές σημειώσεις. Συνειδητοποιεί ότι πολλοί άνθρωποι ψάχνουν το ίδιο πράγμα. Focus: Plural Nouns : How to form the plural of Greek nouns. Plural Articles : Οι (M/F) and Τα (N). Agreement : Matching adjectives to plural nouns. Ανοίγω τα κρ υ φ ά σ υ ρτάρ ια . I open the secret drawers. Οι φωτογραφ ί ες εί ναι παλιές κ αι κίτρινες . The photographs are old and yellow. Υπάρ χ ουν πολλά έγγραφα πάνω στο τραπέζ ι . There are many documents on the table. Οι άνθρωπ οι στην π ό λη εί ναι πολλοί . The people in the city are many. Βλ έπ ω τα ξένα ον ό ματα στη λ ί στα. I see the foreign names on the list. Οι δρ ό μ οι εί ναι επικίνδυνοι τ ώρα . The roads are dangerous now. Τα μ υσ τικ ά εί ναι παντού . The secrets are everywhere. Οι φ ί λ οι μ ου δεν εί ναι εδώ. My friends are not here. Έχο υμε τα στ οι χ εί α π ου θέλ ουμε . We have the clues that we want. 🔍 Reading Analysis In Greek, just like in the singular, Plural Nouns and Adjectives must match in gender. Gender Article (Plural) Noun Ending Example Masculine οι -οι Οι δρόμ οι (The roads) Feminine οι -ες Οι σημειώσ εις (The notes) Neuter τα -α (or -ια) Τα έγγραφ α (The documents) Adjective Agreement (Plural): Masculine: πολλο-ί , επικίνδυνο-ι Feminine: παλι-ές , κίτριν-ες Neuter: πολλ-ά , κρυφ-ά Grammar Hint: Notice that the article οι is used for both Masculine and Feminine plural! However, the noun endings ( -οι vs -ες ) will usually tell you the gender. For neuter nouns, the article τα and the ending -α are the gold standard.

reading-unit-9.title: Reading 9: The Identity of the Man
reading-unit-9.content: Chapter 9: The Identity of the Man (Η Ταυτότητα του Άντρα) NOTE Context / Πλαίσio Nestoras looks closely at the old photograph. He recognizes the eyes and the smile. "This is my grandfather," he says to the woman. She looks at him and reveals that the secret is not just about the city, but about his own blood. Ο Νέστορας κοιτάζει προσεκτικά την παλιά φωτογραφία. Αναγνωρίζει τα μάτια και το χαμόγελο. "Αυτός είναι ο παππούς μου", λέει στη γυναίκα. Εκείνη τον κοιτάζει και αποκαλύπτει ότι το μυστικό δεν είναι μόνο για την πόλη, αλλά για το δικό του αίμα. Focus: Possessive Pronouns : How to say "my", "your", "his/her", etc. Placement : Notice that possessives always follow the noun in Greek. Α υτ ός εί ναι ο πα ππ ούς μου . This is my grandfather. Η ιστορ ί α σου ξεκιν ά ει εδώ. Your story starts here. Ο πατέρας του εί ναι στην εικόνα . His father is in the picture. Η μητέρα της έχει την απάντηση . Her mother has the answer. Το σπίτι μας κρ ύβ ει το μυστ ικ ό . Our house hides the secret. Π ού εί ναι τα κλειδι ά σας ; Where are your keys? Τα ονόματ ά τους εί ναι στη λίστα . Their names are on the list. Η ζω ή σου εί ναι το αίνιγμα . Your life is the enigma. Είμαστε η οικογένει ά σου . We are your family. 🔍 Reading Analysis To show possession in Greek, we use short forms of the personal pronouns after the noun. Meaning Greek Pronoun Example My μου ο παππούς μου Your (sing.) σου η ιστορία σου His / Its του ο πατέρας του Her της η μητέρα της Our μας το σπίτι μας Your (plur./formal) σας τα κλειδιά σας Their τους τα ονόματά τους Grammar Hint: If the noun has an accent on the third-to-last syllable (like οικογένεια ), it gets an extra accent on the last syllable when a possessive pronoun follows! Η οικογένεια → Η οικογένειά μου Το όνομα → Το όνομά σου Το πρόβλημα → Το πρόβλημά μας

reflexive-pronouns.title: Unit 24: Reflexive Pronouns (Myself)
reflexive-pronouns.content: Unit 24: Reflexive Pronouns (Myself) Sometimes in life, the person doing the action and the person receiving the action are the exact same person. In English, we use words ending in "-self" (myself, yourself). In Greek, you have three distinct tools to express this: the pronoun phrase ο εαυτός μου , a Passive verb, or the advanced prefix αυτο- . TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on "Ο εαυτός μου", the difference between emotional and routine reflexives, and the advanced "Αυτο-" prefix. 1. The Core Phrase (Ο εαυτός μου) The word εαυτός (self) is a normal masculine noun. To say "myself," you simply put the definite article in front of it and add a weak possessive pronoun at the end! Person Pronoun Phrase (Accusative) English 1st (Singular) τον εαυτό μου myself 2nd (Singular) τον εαυτό σου yourself 3rd (Singular) τον εαυτό του / της himself / herself TIP Native Secret: The Singular Plural How do you say "ourselves" or "themselves"? While you can make the noun plural (τους εαυτούς μας), native speakers overwhelmingly prefer to leave the word "self" in the singular shape, even when talking about multiple people! Σκέφτονται μόνο τον εαυτό τους . (They only think about themselves / their self ). Πρέπει να προσέχουμε τον εαυτό μας . (We must take care of ourselves ). 2. When to Use It (Emphasis & Emotion) Because εαυτός is a noun, it operates grammatically just like any other direct object in a sentence. It almost always appears in the Accusative case, or right after a preposition. Αγαπώ τον εαυτό μου. (I love myself). Προσέχεις τον εαυτό σου; (Do you take care of yourself?). Πρέπει να βρεις χρόνο για τον εαυτό σου . (You must find time for yourself ). Έχει μεγάλη ιδέα για τον εαυτό της . (She has a very high opinion of herself ). 3. Passive Verbs vs. Pronouns Wait! In Unit 18, we learned that Passive Verbs ending in -ομαι are used to do things to yourself (like taking a bath). So, when do you use the Passive, and when do you use the εαυτός pronoun? 1. The Passive Voice (-ομαι) is for Routine & Grooming: When referring to daily bodily routines (washing, shaving, dressing), Greek strictly uses the passive verb. Ντύνομαι. (I dress myself). Χτενίζομαι. (I comb my hair). 2. The Pronoun Phrase is for Emotion, Damage, & Emphasis: When the action is psychological, damaging, or needs special emphasis, you use the active verb plus the εαυτός phrase. (You generally cannot say "αγαπιέμαι" or "βλάπτομαι" in this context). Μισεί τον εαυτό του. (He hates himself). Καταστρέφεις τον εαυτό σου. (You are destroying yourself). 4. The C1 Advanced "Αυτο-" Prefix There is a final, highly elegant way to express reflexivity. It is heavily used in literature, the news, and academic speech. You can glue the prefix αυτο- directly onto the front of a verb or noun to indicate that the action is directed back at the subject! Η αυτο πεποίθηση (Self-confidence). Η αυτο κόλλητη (Self-adhesive / sticker). Ο άνθρωπος αυτός αυτο καταστράφηκε. (This man self-destructed / destroyed himself). 🎯 Unit 24 Mastery Checklist I can build the phrase for any person (e.g., τον εαυτό σου , τον εαυτό της ). I understand the native habit of keeping εαυτός singular even for plural subjects ( τον εαυτό μας ). I know how to use the phrase after prepositions ( για τον εαυτό μου ). I know that routine grooming uses Passive verbs ( ντύνομαι ), while emotional actions use the pronoun ( αγαπώ τον εαυτό μου ). I recognize the advanced prefix αυτο- (self). Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Next Lesson: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison (Better, Faster, Stronger)

relative-pronouns.title: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (Who, Which, That)
relative-pronouns.content: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (Who, Which, That) Relative pronouns are the glue that links two sentences together. Instead of sounding like a robot ("I saw a man. The man was tall"), you use them to create flow: "I saw a man who was tall." In Greek, you have two tools for this: the easy, casual way, and the formal, precise way. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the frozen "Που" vs. the chameleon "Ο οποίος", and the "Echo Pronoun" trick for clarity. 1. The Easy Way: Που The word που is the ultimate chameleon of the Greek language. It is completely "frozen"—it never changes its shape, regardless of gender, number, or whether you are talking about a human or a chair! It translates to who, which, or that. Ο άντρας που είδα... (The man who/whom I saw...) Το σπίτι που ζήσαμε... (The house that/where we lived...) Η μπύρα που πίνω... (The beer which I am drinking...) CAUTION Spelling Alert (που vs. πού;) Never confuse the relative pronoun που with the question word πού; (Where?). The question word always carries an accent mark; the relative pronoun never does. TIP Native Secret: The "Echo" Pronoun Because που never changes shape, the sentence can sometimes become confusing. To fix this, Greeks frequently add an extra, weak "echo" pronoun inside the second half of the sentence to clarify who is doing what! Η γιατρός που μου τη σύστησες. (The doctor who you recommended her to me). Ο φίλος που του έδωσα το βιβλίο. (The friend who I gave the book to him ). 2. The Formal Way: Ο οποίος If you are writing an essay, giving a speech, or reading the news, you will encounter the formal phrase ο οποίος (who / which). Unlike the frozen που , this phrase is a massive chameleon. It consists of the definite article ( ο, η, το ) plus the word οποίος . It must perfectly match the noun it refers to in Gender and Number! Ο άντρας ο οποίος... (The man who...) Η γυναίκα η οποία... (The woman who...) Τα παιδιά τα οποία... (The children who...) How to say "Whose" (Possession) To say "whose", you simply use the Genitive (Ownership) form of this formal pronoun: του οποίου, της οποίας, των οποίων . Ο φίλος μου, του οποίου η μητέρα είναι δασκάλα... (My friend, whose mother is a teacher...). 3. The Preposition Rule Why do we need both που and ο οποίος ? Because of prepositions! When you need to use a preposition (like "with," "from," or "in") right before the relative pronoun, the formal ο οποίος is strongly preferred in writing. Αυτοί είναι οι άνθρωποι από τους οποίους πήραμε πληροφορίες. (These are the people from whom we got information). Το κτήριο στο οποίο μένω. (The building in which I live). (In casual street speech, Greeks bypass this by using που plus the "Echo" pronoun trick: Το κτήριο που μένω μέσα...). 4. "Whoever" & "Whatever" If you aren't referring to a specific person or thing, you use the "free" relative pronouns: Όποιος / Όποια / Όποιο: Whoever. (e.g., Όποιος θέλει, ας έρθει. - Whoever wants to, let him come). Ό,τι: Whatever / That which. (e.g., Κάνε ό,τι θέλεις. - Do whatever you want). WARNING Spelling Alert (ότι vs. ό,τι) Notice the comma inside ό,τι (whatever). It is absolutely mandatory in writing! It proves you are saying "whatever" and not the conjunction ότι (which just means "that," like Ξέρω ότι είσαι εδώ - I know that you are here). Maximum Emphasis (-δήποτε) If you want to sound incredibly emphatic (Anyone at all! Absolutely anything!), add the suffix -δήποτε . Οποιοσδήποτε: Anyone / Whoever it may be. Οτιδήποτε: Anything / Whatever it may be. 🎯 Unit 23 Mastery Checklist I can use the frozen word που to link two sentences together seamlessly. I know how to use the "echo" pronoun trick ( Ο φίλος που του έδωσα... ). I can recognize the formal chameleon ο οποίος (who/which). I know how to say "whose" by using the genitive ( του οποίου ). I can use όποιος (whoever) and ό,τι (whatever), and I always remember the comma in ό,τι ! Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Next Lesson: Unit 24: Reflexive & Demonstrative Pronouns

simple-stem.title: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem (Σ, Ξ, Ψ)
simple-stem.content: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem (Σ, Ξ, Ψ) To speak dynamically in the Future or the Subjunctive, you need the Simple (Perfective) Stem . This is the "Photo" stem, used exclusively for single, completed actions. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on building the Simple Stem: the Σ-Group, Ξ-Group, Ψ-Group, and the 1-Syllable rebels like "dos" and "pes". Finding this stem is like a puzzle: you look at the last few letters of the verb's continuous stem, drop them, and swap them out for a new sound. There are three main "Cheat Codes" to master for regular Type A verbs. 1. The "S" Group (Σ) ➔ The Most Common If the present tense verb ends in a dental consonant (like -θω ), a nasal consonant (like -νω ), a vowel (like -ούω), or most verbs ending in -ζω , the stem changes to a sharp -σ- (Sigma). διαβάζω (read) ➔ διαβάσ- (θα διαβάσω) κλείνω (close) ➔ κλείσ- (να κλείσω) νιώθω (feel) ➔ νιώσ- (θα νιώσω) ακούω (hear) ➔ ακούσ- (να ακούσω) 2. The "X" Group (Ξ) ➔ The Throat Sounds If the verb ends in a velar "throat" sound like -κω, -γω, -χω, or -σκω , it explodes into a -ξ- (Xi). ανοίγω (open) ➔ ανοίξ- (θα ανοίξω) τρέχω (run) ➔ τρέξ- (να τρέξω) διδάσκω (teach) ➔ διδάξ- (θα διδάξω) WARNING The -ζω Exceptions! While most verbs ending in -ζω go to the "S" group (like διαβάζω -> διαβάσω ), some extremely common ones are rebels and belong to this "X" group. You must memorize these four: παίζω (play) ➔ παίξ- (θα παίξω) αλλάζω (change) ➔ αλλάξ- (να αλλάξω) φωνάζω (shout) ➔ φωνάξ- (θα φωνάξω) κοιτάζω (look) ➔ κοιτάξ- (να κοιτάξω) 3. The "PS" Group (Ψ) ➔ The Lip Sounds If the verb ends in a labial "lip" sound like -πω, -βω, -φω , or -πτω , it merges into a -ψ- (Psi). γράφω (write) ➔ γράψ- (θα γράψω) λείπω (be absent) ➔ λείψ- (να λείψω) κρύβω (hide) ➔ κρύψ- (θα κρύψω) TIP The -εύω / -αύω Rule When a verb ends in -εύω or -αύω , the 'υ' is actually pronounced like an 'f' or 'v' (which are lip sounds). Therefore, these verbs also take the -ψ- ending! δουλεύω (work) ➔ δουλέψ- (θα δουλέψω) χορεύω (dance) ➔ χορέψ- (να χορέψω) 4. Putting it to Work Now that you have the Simple stem (e.g., γράψ- ), you just attach the normal Present tense endings you already know (-ω, -εις, -ει, -ουμε, -ετε, -ουν). Combine this with the particles from the last two units, and you are speaking! Future: Θα γράψω ένα βιβλίο. (I will write a book—once). Subjunctive: Πρέπει να δουλέψω. (I must work—right now). (Remember: the stress mark never moves! If it's on the 'ά' in γράφω, it stays on the 'ά' in γράψω). 5. The "1-Syllable Rebels" (Irregulars) Some verbs refuse to follow any rules. Their perfective stems shrink down to a tiny, single syllable or just one consonant! Because the stem is so short, the stress is forced onto the ending (e.g., θα δ ώ , να π εις ). These are the most common verbs in the language, so memorize them immediately: βλέπω (see) ➔ δ- (θα δώ / να δεις) λέω (say) ➔ π- (θα πώ / να πεις) πίνω (drink) ➔ πι- (θα πιώ / να πιέις) βρίσκω (find) ➔ βρ- (θα βρώ) βγαίνω (go out) ➔ βγ- (θα βγω) μπαίνω (go in) ➔ μπ- (θα μπω) πηγαίνω / πάω (go) ➔ πα- (θα πάω) έρχομαι (come) ➔ ερθ- (θα έρθω) 🎯 Unit 12 Mastery Checklist I can build the -σ- stem for words like διαβάζω and κλείνω . I can build the -ξ- stem for throat sounds, and I remember the παίζω/παίξω exception. I can build the -ψ- stem for lip sounds and -εύω verbs (like δουλεύω -> δουλέψω ). I can combine a Simple Stem with θα or να to make full sentences. I have memorized the "1-Syllable Rebels" like δώ, πώ, βρώ . Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Food & the Restaurant Next Lesson: Unit 13: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns

subjunctive-1.title: Unit 10: Verbal Aspect & The Subjunctive (Να)
subjunctive-1.content: Unit 10: Verbal Aspect & The Subjunctive (Να) Welcome to the great divide in the Greek language. To move from A1 to A2, you must understand a concept called Aspect . TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the "Video vs. Photo" concept (Aspect), the subjunctive "Na" particle, and the rule of negation with Μην. English cares heavily about when an action happens (Past, Present, Future). Greek cares far more about how it happens. This concept is the key to unlocking the entire rest of the language. 1. The "Video vs. Photo" Concept Whenever a Greek person describes an action, their brain forces them to choose between two lenses: The Video (Continuous Aspect): Used for habits, routines, or actions that are currently in progress. You are watching the action happen over time. (e.g., "I play every day" or "I am playing right now"). The Photo (Simple Aspect): Used for a single, specific, or completed event. You view the entire action as a single snapshot. (e.g., "I played one game" or "I want to play once"). Because of this, almost every Greek verb has two different stems : Continuous Stem: The one you already know from the Present tense (e.g., παίζ- ). Simple Stem: A new stem used for single events (e.g., παίξ- ). We will learn exactly how to build this in the next unit, but you need to understand why it exists first! 2. The Subjunctive Mood (The Magic Word: Να) Greek has no "infinitive" form of verbs. You cannot say "I want to play ." Instead, Greek uses a special mood called the Subjunctive. To build it, you use the tiny magic word να (which roughly means "that") followed by a conjugated verb. You literally say: "I want that I play ." English The Greek Logic Greek Translation I want to go. I want that I go . Θέλω να πάω . Do you want to play? Want you that you play ? Θέλεις να παίζεις ; He can help. He can that he helps . Μπορεί να βοηθήσει . When to use Να: After "Want" & "Can": After verbs like θέλω (I want), μπορώ (I can), and πρέπει (I must). As a Command / Wish: You can start a sentence with Να to give an order or offer a wish. ( Να προσέχεις! - Take care! / You should be careful!). To Ask Permission: ( Να μπω; - May I come in?). TIP Bonus Magic Word: Ας (Let's / Let) While να is the standard, Greek also uses ας to express suggestions, permission, or indifference. Ας πάμε! (Let's go!) Ας φύγει. (Let him go / I don't care if he goes.) 3. The Golden Rule of Negation (Μη vs. Δεν) This is one of the most unbreakable rules in Greek grammar: Normal statements (the Indicative mood) are negated with δεν . You learned this in Unit 4. (I do not want = δεν θέλω). However, any verb introduced by να or ας MUST be negated by μη(ν). Θέλω να μην έρθει. (I want him not to come.) Καλύτερα να μην πάμε. (It's better that we don't go.) Ας μην μιλήσουμε γι' αυτό. (Let's not talk about this.) (Note: μη(ν) follows the exact same "dropping 'N'" pronunciation rules you learned for την and δεν!) 4. Bringing It Together: Aspect in the Subjunctive Since να is followed by a verb, you must choose which "Aspect" (Video or Photo) to use for that verb! Because the verb has two stems, you have two choices: Θέλω να παίζω (Continuous/Video Stem) : I want to be playing (regularly, as a hobby, or continuously). Θέλω να παίξω (Simple/Photo Stem) : I want to play (this specific game, right now, just once). IMPORTANT The Secret of the Subjunctive 90% of the time, when you want to do something, or must do something, you are talking about a specific, single event. Therefore, the Simple (Photo) Stem is by far the most commonly used stem after να! 🎯 Unit 10 Mastery Checklist I understand the concept of Aspect: "Video" (Continuous) vs. "Photo" (Simple). I know that Greek has no infinitive and uses να + conjugated verb instead. I remember the unbreakable rule: Verbs after να or ας are ALWAYS negated with μη(ν) , never δεν . I understand why the Simple stem is usually preferred after να . Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Food & Restaurant Next Lesson: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα)

verb-groups-b.title: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2 (The Accented Verbs)
verb-groups-b.content: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2 (The Accented Verbs) In Unit 4, you learned about Type A verbs (like κάνω ). Their defining feature is that the accent stays stubbornly on the stem (κ ά νω, κ ά νεις). TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the "singers" of Greek—Group B verbs—and the two teams: -άω and -ώ. Now, meet Type B (The Second Conjugation) . These are the "singers" of the Greek language. In these verbs, the stress shifts all the way to the end of the word, hitting the final vowels. Because the endings carry the accent, they sound entirely different. Type B verbs are split into two teams: Team B1 and Team B2. 1. Team B1: The "Alpha" Lovers (-άω) This is the largest and most common group of Type B verbs. They are famous for their strong "a" (Alpha) sounds. Examples: αγαπάω (I love), μιλάω (I speak), ρωτάω (I ask), ξυπνάω (I wake up). Person Ending Example: μιλάω (speak) (Εγώ) -άω μιλ άω (Εσύ) -άς μιλ άς (Αυτός/ή/ό) -άει μιλ άει (Εμείς) -άμε μιλ άμε (Εσείς) -άτε μιλ άτε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -άνε μιλ άνε TIP Street Smart vs. Book Smart If you look up these verbs in a formal dictionary, you will see them written as αγαπώ or μιλώ . Greek actually has two sets of endings for these verbs: a formal written set (-ώ, -άς, -ά, -ούμε, -άτε, -ούν) and a casual spoken set (-άω, -άς, -άει, -άμε, -άτε, -άνε). To sound like a natural native speaker, stick to the -άω endings presented in the table above! 2. Team B2: The "Epsilon/Iota" Lovers (-ώ) This team is smaller, but it contains some of the most important verbs in the language, like μπορώ (I can) and ζω (I live). Unlike Team B1, these verbs never use the "Alpha" sound. Instead, they rely on -ει and -ου sounds. Person Ending Example: μπορώ (can) (Εγώ) -ώ μπορ ώ (Εσύ) -είς μπορ είς (Αυτός/ή/ό) -εί μπορ εί (Εμείς) -ούμε μπορ ούμε (Εσείς) -είτε μπορ είτε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ούν μπορ ούν NOTE The Type A Connection! Look closely at the B2 endings. With the exception of "I" ( -ώ ), "We" ( -ούμε ), and "They" ( -ούν ), the endings are exactly the same as the regular Type A verbs you already know ( -εις, -ει, -ετε )! The only difference is that for B2 verbs, you must loudly stress those ending vowels. 3. How to Tell Them Apart? When you encounter a new verb that ends in an accented -ώ : 1. Check if you can add an "a" to it. If the dictionary or a native speaker says μιλάω as well as μιλώ , it belongs to Team B1 . 2. If it sounds completely wrong with an "a" (you can never say μποράω or ζάω), it belongs to Team B2 . CAUTION The Crossovers Greek is flexible. A few verbs actually cross the border! Words like τηλεφωνώ (telephone) and οδηγώ (drive) can be conjugated just like B1 ( τηλεφωνάω, οδηγάνε ) in casual conversation, or like B2 ( τηλεφωνώ, οδηγούν ) in formal settings. When in doubt, you can't go wrong using the formal B2 endings for these crossover words. 🎯 Unit 9 Mastery Checklist I understand that Type B verbs shift their accent to the ending, giving them a distinct rhythm. I can confidently conjugate B1 verbs like μιλάω . I know that using the -άω endings sounds much more natural and conversational than the formal -ώ endings. I can conjugate B2 verbs like μπορώ and notice how their endings resemble Type A. I know how to test whether a verb is B1 or B2 (the "Can I add an Alpha?" test). Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & B-Group Verbs Next Lesson: Unit 10: Verbal Aspect & The Subjunctive (Να)

verb-to-be.title: Unit 3: Being & Having
verb-to-be.content: Unit 3: Being & Having (Είμαι & Έχω) To build real sentences, you need verbs. If you only ever learn two verbs in Greek, make sure they are Είμαι (To Be) and Έχω (To Have). Together, they unlock hundreds of basic conversations and serve as the foundation for complex grammar later on. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on personal pronouns, the "Pro-Drop" rule, and the conjugation of the two most important verbs in Greek. 1. The Actors: Personal Pronouns Before we can use verbs, we need to know the people performing the actions. Here are the Greek personal pronouns: English Greek Pronunciation I Εγώ (e-gó) You (singular/informal) Εσύ (e-sí) He / She / It Αυτός / Αυτή / Αυτό (af-tós / af-tí / af-tó) We Εμείς (e-mís) You (plural or formal) Εσείς (e-sís) They (m / f / n) Αυτοί / Αυτές / Αυτά (af-tí / af-tés / af-tá) TIP Memory Hooks: Notice that Εμείς (We) and Εσείς (You) rhyme completely. The 3rd person pronouns (Αυτός, Αυτή, Αυτό) are highly versatile—they literally mean "this/these" and follow the exact same ending rules as the nouns you learned in Unit 2! 2. Speak Like a Greek: The "Pro-Drop" Rule Here is a massive difference between English and Greek: In English, you must use the pronoun. If you just say "am a student," it sounds broken. You must say " I am a student". In Greek, the end of the verb changes so dramatically for each person that the subject pronoun becomes completely redundant. Greeks drop the pronouns 90% of the time. You only use words like "Εγώ" or "Εσύ" when you want to show contrast or heavy emphasis. Natural: Είμαι εδώ. (I am here.) Emphatic: Εγώ είμαι εδώ! ( I am here, not someone else!). 3. The "Big Two" Verbs Both Είμαι and Έχω are slightly irregular, so they simply need to be memorized. Read them aloud! Είμαι (I Am) Used for identity, state, and location. Person Verb Translation (Εγώ) είμαι I am (Εσύ) είσαι You are (Αυτός/ή/ό) είναι He/She/It is (Εμείς) είμαστε We are (Εσείς) είστε You are (pl./formal) (Αυτοί/ές/ά) είναι They are CAUTION The "Είναι" Trap Did you notice? The word for "He is" ( είναι ) and "They are" ( είναι ) is exactly the same word . You will know which one it is purely from the context of the sentence! Έχω (I Have) Used for possession, age, and family. Person Verb Translation (Εγώ) έχω I have (Εσύ) έχεις You have (Αυτός/ή/ό) έχει He/She/It has (Εμείς) έχουμε We have (Εσείς) έχετε You have (pl./formal) (Αυτοί/ές/ά) έχουν(ε) They have NOTE The Extra Vowel: You will often hear Greeks add an extra "ε" to the end of plural verbs in casual speech. So, έχουν becomes έχουνε . Both are perfectly correct, but έχουνε feels a bit more relaxed. 4. The Formal "You" Greek has a built-in respect system. When speaking to someone older than you, a stranger, or someone in a position of authority (like a doctor or a shop owner), you do not use the singular "Εσύ". Instead, you address them using the plural Εσείς and the plural verb, exactly like the French vous or Spanish usted . Informal (to a friend): Έχεις χρόνο; (Do you have time?) Formal (to a boss): Έχετε χρόνο; (Do you have time?) 5. The Power Combo With these two verbs, you can already speak! Έχεις το βιβλίο; (Do you have the book?) Όχι, ο Γιώργος έχει το βιβλίο. (No, George has the book.) TIP Asking Questions: In English, you change the word order to ask a question ( You have -> Do you have? ). In Greek, the sentence structure stays exactly the same! You simply raise the pitch of your voice at the end of the sentence to show it's a question. And remember, the Greek question mark looks like an English semicolon ( ; ). 🎯 Unit 3 Mastery Checklist I can conjugate είμαι and έχω seamlessly. I understand why Greeks usually drop pronouns like Εγώ and Εσύ . I know that είναι means both "he is" and "they are". I know to use the plural forms ( εσείς, είστε, έχετε ) to show respect to strangers. I know how to turn a statement into a question using just my voice. Ready to practice? A1 Vocabulary: Everyday Objects & Places Next Lesson: Unit 4: Intro to Verbs (Group A)

verbs.title: Unit 4: Regular Verbs & Negation (Type A)
verbs.content: Unit 4: Regular Verbs & Negation (Type A) You’ve learned how to talk about people and things using nouns. Now it’s time to make things happen. Verbs are the engine of every Greek sentence. They change their shape to tell you exactly who is doing the action and when they are doing it. TIP Grammar Podcast (Formal/Academic) Listen to this unit's podcast on the anatomy of Greek verbs, the three verb teams, and the rules for negation with "Δεν" and "Όχι". 1. The Anatomy of a Greek Verb Every Greek verb is built like a puzzle with two pieces: a stem and an ending . Take the verb "to play": παίζω (pé-zo). The Stem is παίζ- (This carries the core meaning: "play"). The Ending is -ω (This tells you who is playing and when ). Almost all Greek verbs you find in a dictionary will end in -ω . This means "I" am doing the action right now. Because the ending tells you exactly who is acting, you usually drop the subject pronoun (Εγώ, Εσύ, etc.), as you learned in Unit 3. 2. The Three Verb "Teams" Before you conjugate a verb, you need to know which "team" it plays for. You can figure this out instantly just by looking at where the accent mark falls in the dictionary word. Type A (First Conjugation): The accent falls on the stem (anywhere before the final -ω). Examples: παίζω (I play), κάνω (I do), γράφω (I write). Type B (Second Conjugation): The accent falls on the final vowel (-ώ). We will cover these in a later unit. Examples: αγαπώ (I love), μιλώ (I speak). Type C (Passive/Deponent): These end in -ομαι . We will tackle these in the intermediate course. In this unit, we are going to master the most common verbs in the language: Type A verbs . 3. Conjugating Type A Verbs (Present Tense) To change the person (I, you, we, etc.), simply chop off the -ω and attach a new ending. Let's conjugate διαβάζω (I read / I study): Pronoun The Ending Full Verb Translation (Εγώ) -ω διαβάζ ω I read (Εσύ) -εις διαβάζ εις You read (Αυτός/ή/ό) -ει διαβάζ ει He/She/It reads (Εμείς) -ουμε διαβάζ ουμε We read (Εσείς) -ετε διαβάζ ετε You read (pl./formal) (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ουν(ε) διαβάζ ουν(ε) They read TIP Stress-Free Stress: The beauty of Type A verbs is that in the present tense, the accent mark NEVER moves. If it's on the 'ά' in διαβ ά ζω, it stays on the 'ά' for every single form (διαβ ά ζουμε, διαβ ά ζετε, etc.). Just lock in the rhythm! NOTE The Extra 'ε': Just like with the verb "to have" (έχουν/έχουνε), Greeks frequently add an extra 'ε' to the 3rd person plural in spoken language ( διαβάζουνε ). It makes the language flow more smoothly. 4. How to Say "No" (Δεν vs. Όχι) In English, turning a positive sentence negative is a clunky process ("I play" -> "I do not play"). In Greek, you simply drop the word δεν (den) right before the verb! Παίζω. (I play.) Δεν παίζω. (I don't play.) Δεν ξέρω. (I don't know.) IMPORTANT Δεν vs. Όχι Never mix these up!
